From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Period of sociopolitical turmoil in China (1966–1976)

This article is about the movement in China. For events elsewhere also called the "Cultural Revolution", see [Cultural Revolution (disambiguation)](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution_(disambiguation) "Cultural Revolution (disambiguation)"). For revolutions in culture generally, see [List of cultural, intellectual, philosophical and technological revolutions](/wiki/List_of_cultural,_intellectual,_philosophical_and_technological_revolutions "List of cultural, intellectual, philosophical and technological revolutions").

Cultural Revolution

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Part of the [History of the People's Republic of China (1949–1976)](/wiki/History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China_(1949%E2%80%931976) "History of the People's Republic of China (1949–1976)") and the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") | |
| Propaganda poster depicting [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong"), above a group of soldiers from the [People's Liberation Army](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army "People's Liberation Army"). The caption reads, "The Chinese People's Liberation Army is the great school of [Mao Zedong Thought](/wiki/Maoism "Maoism")". | |
| Duration | 16 May 1966 – 6 October 1976 (1966-05-16 – 1976-10-06) (10 years and 143 days) |
| Location | [China](/wiki/China "China") |
| Motive | Preservation of communism by purging capitalist and traditional elements, and power struggle between Maoists and pragmatists. |
| Organized by | [Politburo of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Politburo_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Politburo of the Chinese Communist Party") [Cultural Revolution Group](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution_Group "Cultural Revolution Group") |
| Outcome | Economic activity impaired, historical and cultural material destroyed. |
| Deaths | Estimates vary from hundreds of thousands to millions (see *[§ Death toll](#Death_toll)*) |
| Property damage | [Cemetery of Confucius](/wiki/Cemetery_of_Confucius "Cemetery of Confucius"), [Temple of Heaven](/wiki/Temple_of_Heaven "Temple of Heaven"), [Ming tombs](/wiki/Ming_tombs "Ming tombs") |
| Arrests | [Jiang Qing](/wiki/Jiang_Qing "Jiang Qing"), [Zhang Chunqiao](/wiki/Zhang_Chunqiao "Zhang Chunqiao"), [Yao Wenyuan](/wiki/Yao_Wenyuan "Yao Wenyuan"), and [Wang Hongwen](/wiki/Wang_Hongwen "Wang Hongwen") |

| * [v](/wiki/Template:Revolution_sidebar "Template:Revolution sidebar") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Revolution_sidebar "Template talk:Revolution sidebar") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Revolution_sidebar "Special:EditPage/Template:Revolution sidebar") |

The **Cultural Revolution**, formally known as the **Great Proletarian Cultural Revolution**, was a [sociopolitical movement](/wiki/Social_movement "Social movement") in the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/China "China") (PRC). It was launched by [CCP chairman](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Chairman of the Chinese Communist Party") [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong") in 1966 and lasted until [his death](/wiki/Death_and_state_funeral_of_Mao_Zedong "Death and state funeral of Mao Zedong") in 1976. Its stated goal was to preserve [Chinese communism](/wiki/Ideology_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Ideology of the Chinese Communist Party") by purging remnants of [capitalist](/wiki/Capitalism "Capitalism") and [traditional](/wiki/Four_Olds "Four Olds") elements from [Chinese society](/wiki/Chinese_culture "Chinese culture").

In May 1966, with the help of the [Cultural Revolution Group](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution_Group "Cultural Revolution Group"), Mao launched the Revolution and said that [bourgeois elements](/wiki/Bourgeoisie "Bourgeoisie") had infiltrated the government and society with the aim of restoring capitalism. Mao called on young people to [bombard the headquarters](/wiki/Bombard_the_Headquarters "Bombard the Headquarters"), and proclaimed that "to rebel is justified". Mass upheaval began in [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing") with [Red August](/wiki/Red_August "Red August") in 1966. Many young people, mainly students, responded by forming [cadres](/wiki/Cadre_system_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Cadre system of the Chinese Communist Party") of [Red Guards](/wiki/Red_Guards "Red Guards") throughout the country. *[Quotations from Chairman Mao Tse-tung](/wiki/Quotations_from_Chairman_Mao_Tse-tung "Quotations from Chairman Mao Tse-tung")* became revered within [his cult of personality](/wiki/Mao_Zedong%27s_cult_of_personality "Mao Zedong's cult of personality"). In 1967, emboldened radicals began [seizing power](/wiki/Seizure_of_power_(Cultural_Revolution) "Seizure of power (Cultural Revolution)") from local governments and party branches, establishing new [revolutionary committees](/wiki/Revolutionary_committee_(China) "Revolutionary committee (China)") in their place while [smashing public security, procuratorate and judicial systems](/wiki/Smashing_gong-jian-fa "Smashing gong-jian-fa"). These committees often split into rival factions, precipitating [armed clashes among the radicals](/wiki/Violent_Struggle "Violent Struggle"). After the [fall of Lin Biao](/wiki/Lin_Biao_incident "Lin Biao incident") in 1971, the [Gang of Four](/wiki/Gang_of_Four "Gang of Four") became influential in 1972, and the Revolution continued until Mao's death in 1976, soon followed by [the arrest](/wiki/Smashing_the_Gang_of_Four "Smashing the Gang of Four") of the Gang of Four.

The Cultural Revolution was characterized by violence and chaos across Chinese society. Estimates of the death toll vary widely, typically ranging from 1 to 2 million, including [a massacre in Guangxi](/wiki/Guangxi_Massacre "Guangxi Massacre") that included acts of [cannibalism](/wiki/Human_cannibalism "Human cannibalism"), as well as massacres in Beijing, [Inner Mongolia](/wiki/Inner_Mongolia_incident "Inner Mongolia incident"), [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong_Cultural_Revolution_Massacre "Guangdong Cultural Revolution Massacre"), [Yunnan](/wiki/Yunnan_Cultural_Revolution_Massacre_(disambiguation) "Yunnan Cultural Revolution Massacre (disambiguation)"), and [Hunan](/wiki/Hunan_Cultural_Revolution_Massacre_(disambiguation) "Hunan Cultural Revolution Massacre (disambiguation)").[[1]](#cite_note-Song-2011a-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Wang-2001-2) Red Guards sought to destroy the [Four Olds](/wiki/Four_Olds "Four Olds") (old ideas, old culture, old customs, and old habits), which often took the form of destroying historical artifacts and cultural and religious sites. Tens of millions were persecuted, including senior officials such as [Liu Shaoqi](/wiki/Liu_Shaoqi "Liu Shaoqi"), [Deng Xiaoping](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping") and [Peng Dehuai](/wiki/Peng_Dehuai "Peng Dehuai"); millions were persecuted for being members of the [Five Black Categories](/wiki/Five_Black_Categories "Five Black Categories"), with intellectuals and scientists labelled as the [Stinking Old Ninth](/wiki/Stinking_Old_Ninth "Stinking Old Ninth"). The country's schools and universities were closed, and the [National College Entrance Examinations](/wiki/Gaokao "Gaokao") were cancelled. Over 10 million [youth from urban areas](/wiki/Sent-down_youth "Sent-down youth") were relocated under the [Down to the Countryside Movement](/wiki/Down_to_the_Countryside_Movement "Down to the Countryside Movement").

In December 1978, Deng Xiaoping became the new [paramount leader of China](/wiki/Paramount_leader "Paramount leader"), replacing Mao's successor [Hua Guofeng](/wiki/Hua_Guofeng "Hua Guofeng"). Deng and his allies introduced the *[Boluan Fanzheng](/wiki/Boluan_Fanzheng "Boluan Fanzheng")* program and initiated the [Reform and opening up](/wiki/Reform_and_opening_up "Reform and opening up"), which, together with the [New Enlightenment movement](/wiki/New_Enlightenment_(China) "New Enlightenment (China)"), gradually dismantled the ideology of the Cultural Revolution. In 1981, the Communist Party publicly acknowledged numerous failures of the Cultural Revolution, declaring it "responsible for the most severe setback and the heaviest losses suffered by the people, the country, and the party since the founding of the People's Republic." Given its broad scope and social impact, memories and perspectives of the Cultural Revolution are varied and complex in contemporary China. It is often referred to as the "ten years of chaos" (十年动乱; *shí nián dòngluàn*) or "ten years of havoc" (十年浩劫; *shí nián hàojié*).[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The terminology of "cultural revolution" appeared in communist party discourses and newspapers prior to the founding of the People's Republic of China. During this period, the term was used interchangeably with "cultural construction" and referred to the elimination of illiteracy in order to widen public participation in civic matters. This usage of "cultural revolution" continued through the 1950s and into the 1960s, and often involved drawing parallels to the [May Fourth Movement](/wiki/May_Fourth_Movement "May Fourth Movement") or the [Soviet cultural revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution_in_the_Soviet_Union "Cultural Revolution in the Soviet Union") of 1928–1931.[[5]](#cite_note-Thornton2019-5): 56

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Background")]

### Creation of the People's Republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Creation of the People's Republic")]

Main article: [Proclamation of the People's Republic of China](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Proclamation of the People's Republic of China")

On 1 October 1949, Mao Zedong declared the People's Republic of China, symbolically bringing the decades-long [Chinese Civil War](/wiki/Chinese_Civil_War "Chinese Civil War") to a close. Remaining [Republican](/wiki/Republic_of_China "Republic of China") forces fled to [Taiwan](/wiki/Geography_of_Taiwan "Geography of Taiwan") and continued to resist the People's Republic in various ways. Many soldiers of the Chinese Republicans were left in mainland China, and Mao Zedong launched the [Campaign to Suppress Counterrevolutionaries](/wiki/Campaign_to_Suppress_Counterrevolutionaries "Campaign to Suppress Counterrevolutionaries") to eliminate these soldiers left behind, as well as elements of Chinese society viewed as potentially dangerous to Mao's new government.

### Great Leap Forward

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Great Leap Forward")]

Main articles: [Great Leap Forward](/wiki/Great_Leap_Forward "Great Leap Forward") and [Great Chinese Famine](/wiki/Great_Chinese_Famine "Great Chinese Famine")

See also: [Seven Thousand Cadres Conference](/wiki/Seven_Thousand_Cadres_Conference "Seven Thousand Cadres Conference")

This paragraph is an excerpt from [Great Leap Forward](/wiki/Great_Leap_Forward "Great Leap Forward").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Great_Leap_Forward&action=edit)]

The [Great Leap Forward](/wiki/Great_Leap_Forward "Great Leap Forward") was an [industrialization](/wiki/Industrialization "Industrialization") campaign within [China](/wiki/China "China") from 1958 to 1962, led by the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") (CCP). [CCP Chairman](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Chairman of the Chinese Communist Party") [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong") launched the campaign to transform the country from an [agrarian society](/wiki/Agrarian_society "Agrarian society") into an [industrialized society](/wiki/Industrialized_society "Industrialized society") through the formation of [people's communes](/wiki/People%27s_commune "People's commune"). The Great Leap Forward is estimated to have led to between 15 and 55 million deaths in mainland China during the 1959–1961 [Great Chinese Famine](/wiki/Great_Chinese_Famine "Great Chinese Famine") it caused, making it the [largest or second-largest famine](/wiki/List_of_famines "List of famines")[[6]](#cite_note-6) in human history.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-Great_Leap_Forward_Hasell2013-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

On 11 January 1962, an enlarged Central Committee work conference of the CCP was held in Beijing. With over 7,000 participants, it became known as the [Seven Thousand Cadres Conference](/wiki/Seven_Thousand_Cadres_Conference "Seven Thousand Cadres Conference").[[11]](#cite_note-Yang2021ST-11) Liu Shaoqi, Mao Zedong, and others made [self-criticisms](/wiki/Criticism_and_self-criticism_(Marxism%E2%80%93Leninism) "Criticism and self-criticism (Marxism–Leninism)") at the conference. Mao said in the conference that, "Any mistakes that the Centre has made ought to be my direct responsibility, and I also have an indirect share in the blame because I am the Chairman of the Central Committee. I don't want other people to shirk their responsibility. There are some other comrades who also bear responsibility, but the person primarily responsible should be me." He continued: "If our country does not establish a socialist economy, what kind of situation shall we be in? We shall become a country like Yugoslavia, which has actually become a bourgeois country." However, "during the whole socialist stage there still exist classes and class struggle, and this class struggle is a protracted, complex, sometimes even violent affair."[[12]](#cite_note-12)

After the meeting, Liu informed the others that "The errors of the Great Leap Forward were serious, and this is the first time we've summarized the experience. Every year from now on we need to look back and summarize it again." Regarding the cannibalism during the Great Leap Forward, he also remarked, "This will be memorialized as a decree in which the emperor admits his crimes against the people." After the Seven Thousand Cadres Conference, Mao Zedong took a backseat in economic matters.[[11]](#cite_note-Yang2021ST-11)[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 8 However, at the Xilou Conference, Liu Shaoqi still believed that the Seven Thousand Cadres Conference had not sufficiently reflected on the Great Leap Forward.[[14]](#cite_note-Yang2021TPECC-14)

In the first half of 1962, China saw the emergence of a system where individual households were responsible for agricultural output. However, Mao Zedong believed this practice contradicted communism and was something he could not tolerate.[[14]](#cite_note-Yang2021TPECC-14) In July 1962, Mao Zedong expressed dissatisfaction with Liu Shaoqi, stating: "The Three Red Banners have been refuted, land is being divided up, and you did nothing? What will happen after I die?" Liu Shaoqi also argued that "History will record the role you and I played in the starvation of so many people, and the cannibalism will also be memorialized!"[[14]](#cite_note-Yang2021TPECC-14)

### Impact of international tensions and anti-revisionism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Impact of international tensions and anti-revisionism")]

Main article: [Sino-Soviet split](/wiki/Sino-Soviet_split "Sino-Soviet split")

In the early 1950s, the PRC and the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") (USSR) were the world's two largest communist states. Although initially they were mutually supportive, disagreements arose after [Nikita Khrushchev](/wiki/Nikita_Khrushchev "Nikita Khrushchev") took power in the USSR. In 1956, Khrushchev [denounced his predecessor Josef Stalin and his policies](/wiki/On_the_Cult_of_Personality_and_Its_Consequences "On the Cult of Personality and Its Consequences"), and began implementing [economic reforms](/wiki/Khrushchev_Thaw "Khrushchev Thaw"). Mao and many other CCP members opposed these changes, believing that they would damage the worldwide communist movement.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 4–7

Mao believed that Khrushchev was a [revisionist](/wiki/Revisionism_(Marxism) "Revisionism (Marxism)"), altering [Marxist–Leninist](/wiki/Marxism%E2%80%93Leninism "Marxism–Leninism") concepts, which Mao claimed would give capitalists control of the USSR. Relations soured. The USSR refused to support China's case for joining the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") and reneged on its pledge to supply China with a nuclear weapon.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 4–7

Mao denounced revisionism in April 1960. Without pointing at the USSR, Mao criticized its Balkan ally, the [League of Communists of Yugoslavia](/wiki/League_of_Communists_of_Yugoslavia "League of Communists of Yugoslavia"). In turn, the USSR criticized China's Balkan ally, the [Party of Labour of Albania](/wiki/Party_of_Labour_of_Albania "Party of Labour of Albania"). In 1963, CCP began to denounce the USSR, publishing nine polemics.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 7

Other Soviet actions increased concerns about potential [fifth columnists](/wiki/Fifth_columnists "Fifth columnists"). As a result of the tensions following the Sino-Soviet split, Soviet leaders authorized radio broadcasts into China stating that the Soviet Union would assist "genuine communists" who overthrew Mao and his "erroneous course".[[15]](#cite_note-Meyskens2020-15): 141 Chinese leadership also feared the increasing military conflict between the United States and [North Vietnam](/wiki/North_Vietnam "North Vietnam"), concerned that China's support would lead the United States to seek out potential Chinese assets.[[15]](#cite_note-Meyskens2020-15): 141

Mao contended that capitalist tendencies had begun to grow in China.[[16]](#cite_note-:222-16): 151 He viewed the Cultural Revolution as perpetual revolution aimed at opposing "representatives of the bourgeoisie and counterrevolutionary revisionists" who had "sneaked into the party, the government, the army, and cultural circles."[[16]](#cite_note-:222-16): 151–152

The meaning and nature of revolution was a frequent theme in the speeches of central leadership during the Cultural Revolution.[[17]](#cite_note-:232-17): 71 They contended that the Cultural Revolution was an unprecedented event that had to be carried out by the people rather than directed from above and that the best test was revolutionary practice.[[17]](#cite_note-:232-17): 71 [Tao Zhu](/wiki/Tao_Zhu "Tao Zhu") stated:[[17]](#cite_note-:232-17): 71

> You can't say you are the true revolutionaries and they are fake. This is mass organizing. Their organizations may also be revolutionary. You may have a revolutionary competition. Good ones will grow, bad ones will collapse ... True or fake revolutionaries will be distinguished in revolutionary practice.

### Socialist Education Movement and *Hai Rui Dismissed from Office*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Socialist Education Movement and Hai Rui Dismissed from Office")]

See also: [Socialist Education Movement](/wiki/Socialist_Education_Movement "Socialist Education Movement") and [Hai Rui Dismissed from Office](/wiki/Hai_Rui_Dismissed_from_Office "Hai Rui Dismissed from Office")

In 1963, Mao launched the [Socialist Education Movement](/wiki/Socialist_Education_Movement "Socialist Education Movement").[[18]](#cite_note-Baum-1969-18) Mao set the scene by "[cleansing](/wiki/Purge "Purge")" powerful Beijing officials of questionable loyalty. His approach was executed via newspaper articles, internal meetings, and by his network of political allies.[[18]](#cite_note-Baum-1969-18)

In late 1959, historian and deputy mayor of Beijing [Wu Han](/wiki/Wu_Han_(historian) "Wu Han (historian)") published a historical drama entitled *[Hai Rui Dismissed from Office](/wiki/Hai_Rui_Dismissed_from_Office "Hai Rui Dismissed from Office")*. In the play, an honest [civil servant](/wiki/Civil_servant "Civil servant"), [Hai Rui](/wiki/Hai_Rui "Hai Rui"), is dismissed by a corrupt emperor. While Mao initially praised the play, in February 1965, he secretly commissioned [Jiang Qing](/wiki/Jiang_Qing "Jiang Qing") and [Yao Wenyuan](/wiki/Yao_Wenyuan "Yao Wenyuan") to publish an article criticizing it. Yao described the play as an allegory attacking Mao; flagging Mao as the emperor, and Peng Dehuai, who had previously questioned Mao during the [Lushan Conference](/wiki/Lushan_Conference "Lushan Conference"), as the honest civil servant.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 15–18

Yao's article put Beijing mayor [Peng Zhen](/wiki/Peng_Zhen "Peng Zhen") on the defensive. Peng, Wu Han's direct superior, was the head of the [Five Man Group](/wiki/Five_Man_Group "Five Man Group"), a committee commissioned by Mao to study the potential for a cultural revolution. Peng Zhen, aware that he would be implicated if Wu indeed wrote an "anti-Mao" play, wished to contain Yao's influence. Yao's article was initially published only in select local newspapers. Peng forbade its publication in the nationally distributed *[People's Daily](/wiki/People%27s_Daily "People's Daily")* and other major newspapers under his control, and not pay heed to Yao's petty politics.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 14–19 While the "literary battle" against Peng raged, Mao fired [Yang Shangkun](/wiki/Yang_Shangkun "Yang Shangkun")—director of the [party's General Office](/wiki/General_Office_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "General Office of the Chinese Communist Party"), an organ that controlled internal communications—making unsubstantiated charges. He installed loyalist [Wang Dongxing](/wiki/Wang_Dongxing "Wang Dongxing"), head of Mao's security detail. Yang's dismissal likely emboldened Mao's allies to move against their factional rivals.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 14–19

On 12 February 1966, the "Five Man Group" issued a report known as the *February Outline*. The *Outline* as sanctioned by the party center defined *Hai Rui* as a constructive *academic* discussion and aimed to distance Peng Zhen formally from any *political* implications. However, Jiang Qing and Yao Wenyuan continued their denunciations. Meanwhile, Mao sacked [Propaganda Department](/wiki/Propaganda_Department_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Propaganda Department of the Chinese Communist Party") director [Lu Dingyi](/wiki/Lu_Dingyi "Lu Dingyi"), a Peng ally.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 20–27

Lu's removal gave Maoists unrestricted access to the press. Mao delivered his final blow to Peng at a high-profile Politburo meeting through loyalists [Kang Sheng](/wiki/Kang_Sheng "Kang Sheng") and [Chen Boda](/wiki/Chen_Boda "Chen Boda"). They accused Peng of opposing Mao, labeled the *February Outline* "evidence of Peng Zhen's revisionism", and grouped him with three other disgraced officials as part of the "Peng-Luo-Lu-Yang Anti-Party Clique".[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 20–27 On 16 May, the Politburo formalized the decisions by releasing an official document condemning Peng and his "anti-party allies" in the strongest terms, disbanding his "Five Man Group", and replacing it with the Maoist Cultural Revolution Group (CRG).[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 27–35

## 1966: Outbreak

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1966: Outbreak")]

The Cultural Revolution can be divided into two main periods:

* spring 1966 to summer 1968 (when most of the key events took place)
* a tailing period that lasted until fall 1976[[19]](#cite_note-Russo-2020a-19)

The early phase was characterized by mass movement and political pluralization. Virtually anyone could create a political organization, even without party approval. Known as Red Guards, these organizations originally arose in schools and universities and later in factories and other institutions. After 1968, most of these organizations ceased to exist, although their legacies were a topic of controversy later.[[19]](#cite_note-Russo-2020a-19)

### Notification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Notification")]

Main article: [16 May Notification](/wiki/16_May_Notification "16 May Notification")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/%E4%BA%BA%E6%B0%91%E6%97%A5%E6%8A%A5516.jpg/330px-%E4%BA%BA%E6%B0%91%E6%97%A5%E6%8A%A5516.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E4%BA%BA%E6%B0%91%E6%97%A5%E6%8A%A5516.jpg)

The 16 May Notification

In May 1966, an expanded session of the [Politburo](/wiki/Politburo_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Politburo of the Chinese Communist Party") was called in Beijing. The conference was laden with Maoist political rhetoric on [class struggle](/wiki/Class_struggle "Class struggle") and filled with meticulously prepared 'indictments' of recently ousted leaders such as Peng Zhen and [Luo Ruiqing](/wiki/Luo_Ruiqing "Luo Ruiqing"). One of these documents, distributed on 16 May, was prepared with Mao's personal supervision and was particularly damning:[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 39–40

> Those representatives of the bourgeoisie who have sneaked into the Party, the government, the army, and various spheres of culture are a bunch of counter-revolutionary revisionists. Once conditions are ripe, they will seize political power and turn the [dictatorship of the proletariat](/wiki/Dictatorship_of_the_proletariat "Dictatorship of the proletariat") into a dictatorship of the bourgeoisie. Some of them we have already seen through; others we have not. Some are still trusted by us and are being trained as our successors, persons like Khrushchev for example, who are still nestling beside us.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 47

Later known as the "16 May Notification", this document summarized Mao's ideological justification for CR.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 40 Initially kept secret, distributed only among high-ranking party members, it was later declassified and published in *[People's Daily](/wiki/People%27s_Daily "People's Daily")* on 17 May 1967.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 41 Effectively it implied that enemies of the Communist cause could be found within the Party: class enemies who "wave the red flag to oppose the red flag." The only way to identify these people was through "the telescope and microscope of [Mao Zedong Thought](/wiki/Mao_Zedong_Thought "Mao Zedong Thought")."[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 46 While the party leadership was relatively united in approving Mao's agenda, many Politburo members were not enthusiastic, or simply confused about the direction.[[20]](#cite_note-Nianyi-20): 13 The charges against party leaders such as Peng disturbed China's intellectual community and the [eight non-Communist parties](/wiki/United_Front_(China) "United Front (China)").[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 41

### Mass rallies (May–June)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Mass rallies (May–June)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/1966-08_1966%E5%B9%B4%E6%A8%AA%E6%89%AB%E4%B8%80%E5%88%87%E7%89%9B%E9%AC%BC%E8%9B%87%E7%A5%9E.jpg/250px-1966-08_1966%E5%B9%B4%E6%A8%AA%E6%89%AB%E4%B8%80%E5%88%87%E7%89%9B%E9%AC%BC%E8%9B%87%E7%A5%9E.jpg)](/wiki/File:1966-08_1966%E5%B9%B4%E6%A8%AA%E6%89%AB%E4%B8%80%E5%88%87%E7%89%9B%E9%AC%BC%E8%9B%87%E7%A5%9E.jpg)

"[Sweep Away All Cow Demons and Snake Spirits](/wiki/Sweep_Away_All_Cow_Demons_and_Snake_Spirits "Sweep Away All Cow Demons and Snake Spirits")", an editorial published on the front page of *[People's Daily](/wiki/People%27s_Daily "People's Daily")* on 1 June 1966, calling for the proletariat to "completely eradicate" the "[Four Olds](/wiki/Four_Olds "Four Olds") [...] that have poisoned the people of China for thousands of years, fostered by the exploiting classes".[[21]](#cite_note-Gao1987-21): 50

After the purge of Peng Zhen, the Beijing Party Committee effectively ceased to function, paving the way for disorder in the capital. On 25 May, under the guidance of [Cao Yi'ou](/w/index.php?title=Cao_Yi%27ou&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cao Yi'ou (page does not exist)") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%9B%B9%E8%BD%B6%E6%AC%A7 "zh:曹轶欧")]—wife of Mao loyalist Kang Sheng—[Nie Yuanzi](/wiki/Nie_Yuanzi "Nie Yuanzi"), a philosophy lecturer at [Peking University](/wiki/Peking_University "Peking University"), authored a [big-character poster](/wiki/Big-character_poster "Big-character poster") along with other leftists and posted it to a public bulletin. Nie attacked the university's party administration and its leader Lu Ping. Nie insinuated that the university leadership, much like Peng, were trying to contain revolutionary fervor in a "sinister" attempt to oppose the party and advance revisionism.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 56–58

Mao promptly endorsed Nie's poster as "the first Marxist big-character poster in China". Approved by Mao, the poster rippled across educational institutions. Students began to revolt against their school's party establishments. Classes were cancelled in Beijing primary and secondary schools, followed by a decision on 13 June to expand the class suspension nationwide. By early June, throngs of young demonstrators lined the capital's major thoroughfares holding giant portraits of Mao, beating drums, and shouting slogans.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 59–61

When the dismissal of Peng and the municipal party leadership became public in early June, confusion was widespread. The public and foreign missions were kept in the dark on the reason for Peng's ousting. Top Party leadership was caught off guard by the sudden protest wave and struggled with how to respond. After seeking Mao's guidance in [Hangzhou](/wiki/Hangzhou "Hangzhou"), [Liu Shaoqi](/wiki/Liu_Shaoqi "Liu Shaoqi") and [Deng Xiaoping](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping") decided to send in 'work teams'—effectively 'ideological guidance' squads of cadres—to the city's schools and *People's Daily* to restore some semblance of order and re-establish party control.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 62–64

The work teams had a poor understanding of student sentiment. Unlike the political movement of the 1950s that squarely targeted intellectuals, the new movement was focused on established party cadres, many of whom were part of the work teams. As a result, the work teams came under increasing suspicion as thwarting revolutionary fervor.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 71 Party leadership subsequently became divided over whether or not work teams should continue. Liu Shaoqi insisted on continuing work-team involvement and suppressing the movement's most radical elements, fearing that the movement would spin out of control.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 75

### *Bombard the Headquarters* (July)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Bombard the Headquarters (July)")]

Mao–Liu conflict

[![Mao Zedong, Chairman](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/1966-09_1966%E5%B9%B4%E6%AF%9B%E6%B3%BD%E4%B8%9C.jpg/250px-1966-09_1966%E5%B9%B4%E6%AF%9B%E6%B3%BD%E4%B8%9C.jpg)](/wiki/File:1966-09_1966%E5%B9%B4%E6%AF%9B%E6%B3%BD%E4%B8%9C.jpg)

[![Liu Shaoqi, President](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Liu_Shaoqi_%28cropped%29.jpg/120px-Liu_Shaoqi_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Liu_Shaoqi_(cropped).jpg)

In 1966, Mao broke with [Liu Shaoqi](/wiki/Liu_Shaoqi "Liu Shaoqi") (right), then serving as [President](/wiki/President_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "President of the People's Republic of China"), over the work-teams issue. Mao's polemic *[Bombard the Headquarters](/wiki/Bombard_the_Headquarters "Bombard the Headquarters")* was widely recognized as targeting Liu, the purported "bourgeois" party headquarters

Main article: [Bombard the Headquarters](/wiki/Bombard_the_Headquarters "Bombard the Headquarters")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/1966-10_1966%E5%B9%B4%E6%AF%9B%E6%B3%BD%E4%B8%9C%E6%B8%B8%E9%95%BF%E6%B1%9F2.jpg/330px-1966-10_1966%E5%B9%B4%E6%AF%9B%E6%B3%BD%E4%B8%9C%E6%B8%B8%E9%95%BF%E6%B1%9F2.jpg)](/wiki/File:1966-10_1966%E5%B9%B4%E6%AF%9B%E6%B3%BD%E4%B8%9C%E6%B8%B8%E9%95%BF%E6%B1%9F2.jpg)

Mao waves to the crowd on the banks of the Yangtze before his swim across, July 1966

In July, Mao, in Wuhan, crossed the Yangtze River, showing his vigor. He then returned from Wuhan to Beijing and criticized party leadership for its handling of the work-teams issue. Mao accused the work teams of undermining the student movement, calling for their full withdrawal on 24 July. Several days later a rally was held at the [Great Hall of the People](/wiki/Great_Hall_of_the_People "Great Hall of the People") to announce the decision and reveal the tone of the movement to teachers and students. At the rally, Party leaders encouraged the masses to 'not be afraid' and take charge of the movement, free of Party interference.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 81–84

The work-teams issue marked a decisive defeat for Liu; it also signaled that disagreement over how to handle the CR's unfolding events would irreversibly split Mao from the party leadership. On 1 August, the Eleventh Plenum of the [8th Central Committee](/wiki/8th_Central_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "8th Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party") was convened to advance Mao's radical agenda. At the plenum, Mao showed disdain for Liu, repeatedly interrupting him as he delivered his opening day speech.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 94

Red Guards in Beijing

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/1967-11_1967%E5%B9%B4_%E5%8C%97%E4%BA%AC%E5%B8%88%E8%8C%83%E5%A4%A7%E5%AD%A6%E5%A4%A7%E5%AD%97%E6%8A%A5%E6%89%B9%E8%AF%84%E5%88%98%E5%B0%91%E5%A5%87.jpg/250px-1967-11_1967%E5%B9%B4_%E5%8C%97%E4%BA%AC%E5%B8%88%E8%8C%83%E5%A4%A7%E5%AD%A6%E5%A4%A7%E5%AD%97%E6%8A%A5%E6%89%B9%E8%AF%84%E5%88%98%E5%B0%91%E5%A5%87.jpg)](/wiki/File:1967-11_1967%E5%B9%B4_%E5%8C%97%E4%BA%AC%E5%B8%88%E8%8C%83%E5%A4%A7%E5%AD%A6%E5%A4%A7%E5%AD%97%E6%8A%A5%E6%89%B9%E8%AF%84%E5%88%98%E5%B0%91%E5%A5%87.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/1967-11_1967%E5%B9%B4_%E5%8C%97%E4%BA%AC%E5%A4%A7%E5%AD%A6%E5%A4%A7%E5%AD%97%E6%8A%A5.jpg/250px-1967-11_1967%E5%B9%B4_%E5%8C%97%E4%BA%AC%E5%A4%A7%E5%AD%A6%E5%A4%A7%E5%AD%97%E6%8A%A5.jpg)](/wiki/File:1967-11_1967%E5%B9%B4_%E5%8C%97%E4%BA%AC%E5%A4%A7%E5%AD%A6%E5%A4%A7%E5%AD%97%E6%8A%A5.jpg)

From left: (1) Students at [Beijing Normal University](/wiki/Beijing_Normal_University "Beijing Normal University") making big-character posters denouncing [Liu Shaoqi](/wiki/Liu_Shaoqi "Liu Shaoqi"); (2) Big-characters posted at [Peking University](/wiki/Peking_University "Peking University"); (3) Students at No. 23 Middle School in Beijing reading *[People's Daily](/wiki/People%27s_Daily "People's Daily")* during the "Resume Classes" campaign

On 28 July, [Red Guard](/wiki/Red_Guards "Red Guards") representatives wrote to Mao, calling for rebellion and upheaval to safeguard the revolution. Mao then responded to the letters by writing his own big-character poster entitled *[Bombard the Headquarters](/wiki/Bombard_the_Headquarters "Bombard the Headquarters")*, rallying people to target the "command centre (i.e., Headquarters) of counterrevolution." Mao wrote that despite having undergone a communist revolution, a "bourgeois" elite was still thriving in "positions of authority" in the government and Party.[[22]](#cite_note-Tang-22)

This statement has been interpreted as a direct indictment of the party establishment under Liu and Deng—the purported "bourgeois headquarters" of China. The personnel changes at the Plenum reflected a radical re-design of the party hierarchy. Liu and Deng kept their seats on the Politburo Standing Committee, but were sidelined from day-to-day party affairs. Lin Biao was elevated to become the CCP's number-two; Liu's rank went from second to eighth and was no longer Mao's heir apparent.[[22]](#cite_note-Tang-22)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Guangmei_cultural_revolution.jpg/250px-Guangmei_cultural_revolution.jpg)](/wiki/File:Guangmei_cultural_revolution.jpg)

A [struggle session](/wiki/Struggle_session "Struggle session") targeting Liu Shaoqi's wife [Wang Guangmei](/wiki/Wang_Guangmei "Wang Guangmei")

Along with the top leadership losing power the entire national Party bureaucracy was purged. The extensive [Organization Department](/wiki/Organization_Department_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Organization Department of the Chinese Communist Party"), in charge of party personnel, virtually ceased to exist. The top officials in the Propaganda Department were sacked, with many of its functions folded into the CRG.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 96

### Red August and the Sixteen Points

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Red August and the Sixteen Points")]

Main article: [Red August](/wiki/Red_August "Red August")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/1966-11_1966%E5%B9%B4%E6%AF%9B%E6%B3%BD%E4%B8%9C%E6%9E%97%E5%BD%AA%E4%B8%8E%E7%BA%A2%E5%8D%AB%E5%85%B5.jpg/250px-1966-11_1966%E5%B9%B4%E6%AF%9B%E6%B3%BD%E4%B8%9C%E6%9E%97%E5%BD%AA%E4%B8%8E%E7%BA%A2%E5%8D%AB%E5%85%B5.jpg)](/wiki/File:1966-11_1966%E5%B9%B4%E6%AF%9B%E6%B3%BD%E4%B8%9C%E6%9E%97%E5%BD%AA%E4%B8%8E%E7%BA%A2%E5%8D%AB%E5%85%B5.jpg)

Mao and [Lin Biao](/wiki/Lin_Biao "Lin Biao") surrounded by rallying Red Guards in Beijing, December 1966

*[Quotations from Chairman Mao Tse-tung](/wiki/Quotations_from_Chairman_Mao_Tse-tung "Quotations from Chairman Mao Tse-tung")* led the Red Guards to commit to their objective as China's future.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 107 By December 1967, 350 million copies had been printed.[[23]](#cite_note-Lu-23): 61–64

During the Red August of Beijing, on 8 August 1966, the [party's General Committee](/wiki/Central_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party") passed its "Decision Concerning the Great Proletarian Cultural Revolution," later to be known as the "Sixteen Points". This decision defined the Cultural Revolution as "a great revolution that touches people to their very souls and constitutes a new stage in the development of the socialist revolution in our country:"[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 92–93

> Although the bourgeoisie has been overthrown, it is still trying to use the old ideas, culture, customs and habits of the exploiting classes to corrupt the masses, capture their minds and endeavour to stage a comeback. The proletariat must do the exact opposite: it must meet head-on every challenge of the bourgeoisie ... to change the mental outlook of the whole of society. At present, our objective is to struggle against and overthrow those persons in authority who are taking the capitalist road, to criticize and repudiate the reactionary bourgeois academic "authorities" and the ideology of the bourgeoisie and all other exploiting classes and to transform education, literature and art and all other parts of the superstructure not in correspondence with the socialist economic base, so as to facilitate the consolidation and development of the socialist system.

The implications of the Sixteen Points were far-reaching. It elevated what was previously a student movement to a nationwide mass campaign that would galvanize workers, farmers, soldiers and lower-level party functionaries to rise, challenge authority, and re-shape the [superstructure](/wiki/Superstructure_(Marxism) "Superstructure (Marxism)") of society.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/1966-11_1966%E5%B9%B49%E6%9C%8815%E6%97%A5%E5%A4%A9%E5%AE%89%E9%97%A8%E6%B8%B8%E8%A1%8C-%E5%85%AB%E5%B1%8A%E5%8D%81%E4%B8%80%E4%B8%AD.jpg/250px-1966-11_1966%E5%B9%B49%E6%9C%8815%E6%97%A5%E5%A4%A9%E5%AE%89%E9%97%A8%E6%B8%B8%E8%A1%8C-%E5%85%AB%E5%B1%8A%E5%8D%81%E4%B8%80%E4%B8%AD.jpg)](/wiki/File:1966-11_1966%E5%B9%B49%E6%9C%8815%E6%97%A5%E5%A4%A9%E5%AE%89%E9%97%A8%E6%B8%B8%E8%A1%8C-%E5%85%AB%E5%B1%8A%E5%8D%81%E4%B8%80%E4%B8%AD.jpg)

Tiananmen Square on 15 September 1966, the occasion of Chairman Mao's third of eight mass rallies with Red Guards in 1966.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

On 18 August in Beijing, over a million Red Guards from across the country gathered in and around [Tiananmen Square](/wiki/Tiananmen_Square "Tiananmen Square") for an audience with the chairman.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 106–107 Mao mingled with Red Guards and encouraged them, donning a Red Guard armband. Lin also took centre stage, denouncing perceived enemies in society that were impeding the "progress of the revolution".[[20]](#cite_note-Nianyi-20): 66 Subsequently, violence escalated in Beijing and quickly spread.[[2]](#cite_note-Wang-2001-2)[[26]](#cite_note-Jian-2006-26): xvi The 18 August rally was filmed and shown to approximately 100 million people in its first month of release.[[27]](#cite_note-Li2023-27): 53

On 22 August, a central directive was issued to prevent police intervention in Red Guard activities, and those in the police force who defied this notice were labeled counter-revolutionaries. Central officials lifted restraints on violent behavior. [Xie Fuzhi](/wiki/Xie_Fuzhi "Xie Fuzhi"), the national police chief, often pardoned Red Guards for their "crimes".[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 124–126

The campaign included incidents of torture, murder, and public humiliation. Many people who were indicted as counter-revolutionaries died by suicide. During Red August, 1,772 people were murdered in Beijing; many of the victims were teachers who were attacked or killed by their own students.[[2]](#cite_note-Wang-2001-2) In September, Shanghai experienced 704 suicides and 534 deaths; in Wuhan, 62 suicides and 32 murders occurred during the same period.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 124 Peng Dehuai was brought to Beijing to be publicly ridiculed.

### Destruction of the Four Olds (August–November)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Destruction of the Four Olds (August–November)")]

Main article: [Four Olds](/wiki/Four_Olds "Four Olds")

See also: [Great Exchange of Revolutionary Experience](/wiki/Great_Exchange_of_Revolutionary_Experience "Great Exchange of Revolutionary Experience")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Wanlitod.jpg/250px-Wanlitod.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wanlitod.jpg)

The remains of the [Wanli Emperor](/wiki/Wanli_Emperor "Wanli Emperor") at the Ming tombs. Red Guards dragged the remains of the Wanli Emperor and Empresses to the front of the tomb, where they were posthumously "denounced" and burned[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Between August and November 1966, eight mass rallies were held, drawing in 12 million people, most of whom were Red Guards.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 106 To aid Red Guards in traveling, the [Great Exchange of Revolutionary Experience](/wiki/Great_Exchange_of_Revolutionary_Experience "Great Exchange of Revolutionary Experience") program, which lasted from September 1966 to early 1967, gave them free food and lodging throughout the country.[[29]](#cite_note-Singer-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 110–113

At the rallies, [Lin Biao](/wiki/Lin_Biao "Lin Biao") called for the destruction of the Four Olds; namely, old customs, culture, habits, and ideas.[[20]](#cite_note-Nianyi-20): 66[[31]](#cite_note-:022-31): 146 Some changes associated with the Four Olds campaign were mainly benign, such as assigning new names to city streets, places, and even people; millions of babies were born with "revolutionary" names.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Other aspects were more destructive, particularly in the realms of culture and religion. Historical sites throughout the country were destroyed. The damage was particularly pronounced in the capital, Beijing. Red Guards laid siege to the [Temple of Confucius](/wiki/Temple_of_Confucius,_Qufu "Temple of Confucius, Qufu") in [Qufu](/wiki/Qufu "Qufu"),[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 119 and other historically significant tombs and artifacts.[[33]](#cite_note-Asiaweek,_Volume_10-33)

Libraries of historical and foreign texts were destroyed; books were burned. Temples, churches, mosques, monasteries, and cemeteries were closed and sometimes converted to other uses, or looted and destroyed.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Marxist propaganda depicted [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") as superstition, and religion was looked upon as a means of hostile foreign infiltration, as well as an instrument of the ruling class.[[35]](#cite_note-Dan_Smyer_2007-35) Clergy were arrested and sent to camps; many [Tibetan Buddhists](/wiki/Tibetan_Buddhism "Tibetan Buddhism") were forced to participate in the destruction of their monasteries at gunpoint.[[35]](#cite_note-Dan_Smyer_2007-35)

  The [cemetery of Confucius](/wiki/Cemetery_of_Confucius "Cemetery of Confucius") was attacked by Red Guards in November 1966.[[33]](#cite_note-Asiaweek,_Volume_10-33)[[36]](#cite_note-Jeni_Hung-36)

A metal replica is in its place.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Statue_of_Emperor_-_Ming_Tombs.jpg/120px-Statue_of_Emperor_-_Ming_Tombs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Statue_of_Emperor_-_Ming_Tombs.jpg "This statue of the Yongle Emperor was originally carved in stone, and was destroyed in the Cultural Revolution. A metal replica is in its place.")

  This statue of the [Yongle Emperor](/wiki/Yongle_Emperor "Yongle Emperor") was originally carved in stone, and was destroyed in the Cultural Revolution. A metal replica is in its place.
* [![The remains of the 8th century Buddhist monk Huineng were attacked during the Cultural Revolution.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Huineng.jpg/120px-Huineng.jpg)](/wiki/File:Huineng.jpg "The remains of the 8th century Buddhist monk Huineng were attacked during the Cultural Revolution.")

  The remains of the 8th century Buddhist monk [Huineng](/wiki/Huineng "Huineng") were attacked during the Cultural Revolution.
* [![A frieze damaged during the Cultural Revolution, originally from a garden house of a rich imperial official in Suzhou.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/SuzhouGardenFrieze.jpg/120px-SuzhouGardenFrieze.jpg)](/wiki/File:SuzhouGardenFrieze.jpg "A frieze damaged during the Cultural Revolution, originally from a garden house of a rich imperial official in Suzhou.")

  A frieze damaged during the Cultural Revolution, originally from a garden house of a rich imperial official in Suzhou.

In September 1966, central Party authorities under Zhou Enlai issued the *Instructions on Grasping Revolution, Promoting Production*, which directed that "one must grasp revolution on one hand and promote production on the other hand.[[37]](#cite_note-:02222-37): 251

### Central Work Conference (October)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Central Work Conference (October)")]

In October 1966, Mao convened a Central Work Conference, mostly to enlist party leaders who had not yet adopted the latest ideology. Liu and Deng were prosecuted and begrudgingly offered self-criticism.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 137 After the conference, Liu, once a powerful moderate pundit, was placed under house arrest, then sent to a detention camp, where he was denied medical treatment and died in 1969. Deng was sent away for a period of re-education three times and was eventually sent to work in an engine factory in [Jiangxi](/wiki/Jiangxi "Jiangxi"). Rebellion by [party cadres](/wiki/Cadre_system_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Cadre system of the Chinese Communist Party") accelerated after the conference.[[38]](#cite_note-Walder-2016-38)

### End of the year

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: End of the year")]

On 5 October, the [Central Military Commission](/wiki/Central_Military_Commission_(China) "Central Military Commission (China)") and the PLA's Department of General Political Tasks directed military academies to dismiss their classes to allow cadets to become more involved in the Cultural Revolution.[[31]](#cite_note-:022-31): 147 In doing so, they were acting on Lin Biao's 23 August 1966 for "three month turmoil" in the PLA.[[31]](#cite_note-:022-31): 147

In [Macau](/wiki/Portuguese_Macau "Portuguese Macau"), rioting broke out during the [12-3 incident](/wiki/12-3_incident "12-3 incident").[[39]](#cite_note-Simpson2023-39): 84 The event was prompted by the colonial government's delays in approving a new wing for a CCP elementary school in [Taipa](/wiki/Taipa "Taipa").[[39]](#cite_note-Simpson2023-39): 84 The school board illegally began construction, but the colonial government sent police to stop the workers. Several people were injured in the resulting [melee](/wiki/Melee "Melee"). On 3 December 1966, two days of rioting occurred in which hundreds were injured and six to eight were killed, leading to a total clampdown by the Portuguese government.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The event set in motion Portugal's de facto abdication of control over Macau, putting Macau on the path to eventual absorption by China.[[39]](#cite_note-Simpson2023-39): 84–85

By the beginning of 1967, a wide variety of grassroots political organizations had formed. Beyond Red Guard and student rebel groups, these included poor peasant associations, workers' pickets, and Mao Zedong Thought study societies, among others. Communist Party leaders encouraged these groups to "join up", and these groups joined various coalitions and held various cross-group congresses and assemblies.[[5]](#cite_note-Thornton2019-5): 60

## 1967: Seizure of power

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 1967: Seizure of power")]

Main article: [Seizure of power (Cultural Revolution)](/wiki/Seizure_of_power_(Cultural_Revolution) "Seizure of power (Cultural Revolution)")

See also: [Violent struggle](/wiki/Violent_struggle "Violent struggle"), [Rebel Faction (Cultural Revolution)](/wiki/Rebel_Faction_(Cultural_Revolution) "Rebel Faction (Cultural Revolution)"), [Conservative Faction (Cultural Revolution)](/wiki/Conservative_Faction_(Cultural_Revolution) "Conservative Faction (Cultural Revolution)"), and [Smashing gong-jian-fa](/wiki/Smashing_gong-jian-fa "Smashing gong-jian-fa")

Mass organizations coalesced into two factions, the radicals who backed Mao's purge of the Communist party, and the conservatives who backed the moderate party establishment. The "support the left" policy was established in January 1967.[[41]](#cite_note-Tanigawa-2018-41) Mao's policy was to support the rebels in seizing power; it required the PLA to support "the broad masses of the revolutionary leftists in their struggle to seize power."[[41]](#cite_note-Tanigawa-2018-41)

In March 1967, the policy was adapted into the "Three Supports and Two Militaries" initiative, in which PLA troops were sent to schools and work units across the country to stabilize political tumult and end factional warfare.[[42]](#cite_note-Xu2022-42): 345 The three "Supports" were to "support the left", "support the interior", "support industry". The "two Militaries" referred to "military management" and "military training".[[42]](#cite_note-Xu2022-42): 345 The policy of supporting the left failed to define "leftists" at a time when almost all mass organizations claimed to be "leftist" or "revolutionary".[[41]](#cite_note-Tanigawa-2018-41) PLA commanders had developed close working relations with the party establishment, leading many military units to repress radicals.[[43]](#cite_note-war-43)

Spurred by the events in Beijing, [power seizure groups](/wiki/Seizure_of_power_(Cultural_Revolution) "Seizure of power (Cultural Revolution)") formed across the country and began expanding into factories and the countryside. In Shanghai, a young factory worker named [Wang Hongwen](/wiki/Wang_Hongwen "Wang Hongwen") organized a far-reaching revolutionary coalition, one that displaced existing Red Guard groups. On 3 January 1967, with support from CRG heavyweights Zhang Chunqiao and Yao Wenyuan, the group of firebrand activists overthrew the Shanghai municipal government under [Chen Pixian](/wiki/Chen_Pixian "Chen Pixian") in what became known as the [January Storm](/wiki/January_Storm "January Storm"), and formed in its place the [Shanghai People's Commune](/wiki/Shanghai_People%27s_Commune "Shanghai People's Commune").[[44]](#cite_note-Jiaqi-44)[[23]](#cite_note-Lu-23): 115 Mao then expressed his approval.[[38]](#cite_note-Walder-2016-38)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/1967-04_1967%E5%B9%B4%E4%B8%8A%E6%B5%B7%E9%80%A0%E5%8F%8D%E6%B4%BE%E5%BA%86%E7%A5%9D%E6%B8%B8%E8%A1%8C.jpg/250px-1967-04_1967%E5%B9%B4%E4%B8%8A%E6%B5%B7%E9%80%A0%E5%8F%8D%E6%B4%BE%E5%BA%86%E7%A5%9D%E6%B8%B8%E8%A1%8C.jpg)](/wiki/File:1967-04_1967%E5%B9%B4%E4%B8%8A%E6%B5%B7%E9%80%A0%E5%8F%8D%E6%B4%BE%E5%BA%86%E7%A5%9D%E6%B8%B8%E8%A1%8C.jpg)

[Rebel factions](/wiki/Rebel_faction_(Cultural_Revolution) "Rebel faction (Cultural Revolution)") of Red Guards marching in Shanghai, 1967

Shanghai's was the first provincial level government overthrown.[[38]](#cite_note-Walder-2016-38) Provincial governments and many parts of the state and party bureaucracy were affected, with power seizures taking place. In the next three weeks, 24 more province-level governments were overthrown.[[38]](#cite_note-Walder-2016-38) ["Revolutionary committees"](/wiki/Revolutionary_committee_(China) "Revolutionary committee (China)") were subsequently established, in place of local governments and branches of the Communist Party.[[45]](#cite_note-45) For example, in Beijing, three separate revolutionary groups declared power seizures on the same day. In Heilongjiang, local party secretary [Pan Fusheng](/wiki/Pan_Fusheng "Pan Fusheng") seized power from the party organization under his own leadership. Some leaders even wrote to the CRG asking to be overthrown.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 170–72

In Beijing, Jiang Qing and Zhang Chunqiao targeted Vice-Premier [Tao Zhu](/wiki/Tao_Zhu "Tao Zhu"). The power-seizure movement was appearing in the military as well. In February, prominent generals [Ye Jianying](/wiki/Ye_Jianying "Ye Jianying") and [Chen Yi](/wiki/Chen_Yi_(general) "Chen Yi (general)"), as well as Vice-Premier [Tan Zhenlin](/wiki/Tan_Zhenlin "Tan Zhenlin"), vocally asserted their opposition to the more extreme aspects of the movement, with some party elders insinuating that the CRG's real motives were to remove the revolutionary old guard. Mao, initially ambivalent, took to the Politburo floor on 18 February to denounce the opposition directly, endorsing the radicals' activities. This resistance was branded the [February Countercurrent](/wiki/February_Countercurrent "February Countercurrent")[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 195–196—effectively silencing critics within the party.[[20]](#cite_note-Nianyi-20): 207–209

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/1967-04_%E8%B4%B5%E5%B7%9E%E9%9D%A9%E5%91%BD%E6%B4%BE%E9%80%A0%E5%8F%8D.jpg/250px-1967-04_%E8%B4%B5%E5%B7%9E%E9%9D%A9%E5%91%BD%E6%B4%BE%E9%80%A0%E5%8F%8D.jpg)](/wiki/File:1967-04_%E8%B4%B5%E5%B7%9E%E9%9D%A9%E5%91%BD%E6%B4%BE%E9%80%A0%E5%8F%8D.jpg)

Red Guards marching in [Guizhou](/wiki/Guizhou "Guizhou"), 1967. The banner in the center reads: "The People's Liberation Army firmly supports the proletarian revolutionary faction."

Although in early 1967 popular insurgencies were limited outside of the biggest cities, local governments began collapsing all across China.[[46]](#cite_note-Walder2019b-46): 21 Revolutionaries dismantled ruling government and party organizations. Because power seizures lacked centralized leadership, it was no longer clear who believed in Mao's revolutionary vision and who was exploiting the chaos for their own gain. The formation of rival revolutionary groups and manifestations of long-established local feuds led to [violent struggles](/wiki/Violent_Struggle "Violent Struggle") between factions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Tension grew between mass organizations and the military. In response, Lin Biao issued a directive for the army to aid the radicals. At the same time, the army took control of some provinces and locales that were deemed incapable of handling the power transition.[[20]](#cite_note-Nianyi-20): 219–221

In Wuhan, as in many other cities, two major revolutionary organizations emerged, one supporting and one attacking the conservative establishment. [Chen Zaidao](/wiki/Chen_Zaidao "Chen Zaidao"), the Army general in charge of the area, forcibly repressed the anti-establishment demonstrators. Mao flew to Wuhan with a large entourage of central officials in an attempt to secure military loyalty in the area. On 20 July 1967, local agitators in response kidnapped Mao's emissary [Wang Li](/wiki/Wang_Li_(politician) "Wang Li (politician)"), in what became known as the [Wuhan Incident](/wiki/Wuhan_Incident "Wuhan Incident"). Subsequently, Chen was sent to Beijing and tried by Jiang Qing and the rest of the CRG. Chen's resistance was the last major open display of opposition within the PLA.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 214

The Gang of Four's [Zhang Chunqiao](/wiki/Zhang_Chunqiao "Zhang Chunqiao") admitted that the most crucial factor in the Cultural Revolution was not the Red Guards or the CRG or the "rebel worker" organisations, but the PLA. When the PLA local garrison supported Mao's radicals, they were able to take over the local government successfully, but if they were not cooperative, the takeovers were unsuccessful.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 175 Violent clashes occurred in virtually all major cities.[[1]](#cite_note-Song-2011a-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Wang-2001-2)

In response to the Wuhan Incident, Mao and Jiang began establishing a "workers' armed self-defense force", a "revolutionary armed force of mass character" to counter what he saw as rightism in "75% of the PLA officer corps". Meanwhile, a massive movement to "[smash gong-jian-fa](/wiki/Smashing_gong-jian-fa "Smashing gong-jian-fa")", or to smash the Police, the Procuratorate and the Court, was carried out in mainland China.[[47]](#cite_note-47) The few remaining going-jian-fa organizations were later placed under military control.[[48]](#cite_note-:8-48)

Some locations of armed conflict between rebel factions during the summer of 1967.

In [Chongqing](/wiki/Chongqing "Chongqing"), factional violence was particularly pronounced.[[49]](#cite_note-:023-49): 178 Violence there was exacerbated because the city had a concentration of munitions factories.[[50]](#cite_note-:Li2-50): 336 Violence in Chongqing occurred primarily between two different rebel factions during the period 16 May 1967 to 15 October 1968.[[17]](#cite_note-:232-17): 62–63 Among the major instances of combat there was the 25 July Incident in 1967, during which members of one rebel faction attacked four hundred members of other factions using knives, pistols, rifles, submachine guns, and machine guns, killing ten.[[17]](#cite_note-:232-17): 64

[Unconventional weapons](/wiki/Unconventional_warfare "Unconventional warfare"), including [weapon of mass destruction](/wiki/Weapon_of_mass_destruction "Weapon of mass destruction"), were seized during conflicts, but not directly used. Citizens wrote letters to the [Zhongnanhai](/wiki/Zhongnanhai "Zhongnanhai") residence of government leaders, warning of attacks on facilities that stored [pathogenic bacteria](/wiki/Pathogenic_bacteria "Pathogenic bacteria"), [poisonous plant](/wiki/Poisonous_plant "Poisonous plant") samples, radioactive substances, poison gas, toxicants, and other dangerous substances. In [Changchun](/wiki/Changchun "Changchun"), rebels working in geological institutes developed and tested a [dirty bomb](/wiki/Dirty_bomb "Dirty bomb"), a crude [radiological weapon](/wiki/Radiological_weapon "Radiological weapon"), testing two "radioactive self-defense bombs" and two "radioactive self-defense mines" on 6 and 11 August.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 218–220

Violence in 1967 disrupted economic activity and touring Red Guards overburdened China's transportation system.[[37]](#cite_note-:02222-37): 251–252 By year end, national industrial output had decreased by 13.8% from the previous year.[[37]](#cite_note-:02222-37): 252

Military control was imposed over the [Daqing Oil Field](/wiki/Daqing_Oil_Field "Daqing Oil Field") in March 1967 and over the [Anshan Iron and Steel Plant](/wiki/Anshan_Iron_%26_Steel "Anshan Iron & Steel") in August.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 214–217

Nationwide, a total of 18.77 million firearms, 14,828 artillery pieces, 2,719,545 grenades ended up in civilian hands. They were used in the course of violent struggles, which mostly took place from 1967 to 1968. In Chongqing, [Xiamen](/wiki/Xiamen "Xiamen"), and [Changchun](/wiki/Changchun "Changchun"), tanks, armored vehicles and even warships were deployed in combat.[[43]](#cite_note-war-43)

In late 1967, the PLA became the most powerful political force in the country.[[37]](#cite_note-:02222-37): 253 In 1967 and 1968, rebel groups supported by the PLA established [Revolutionary Committees](/wiki/Revolutionary_committee_(China) "Revolutionary committee (China)") that replaced government and existing Party organizations at the local and provincial levels.[[37]](#cite_note-:02222-37): 253

During the Cultural Revolution, Mao emphasized the need to improve medical care in rural China.[[51]](#cite_note-:0222-51): 270 The Rural Cooperative Medical System (RCMS) developed in the late 1960s.[[51]](#cite_note-:0222-51): 270 In this system, each large production brigade established a medical cooperative station staffed by [barefoot doctors](/wiki/Barefoot_doctor "Barefoot doctor").[[51]](#cite_note-:0222-51): 270 The medical cooperative stations provided primary health care.[[51]](#cite_note-:0222-51): 270 [Barefoot doctors](/wiki/Barefoot_doctor "Barefoot doctor") brought healthcare to rural areas where urban-trained doctors would not settle. They promoted basic [hygiene](/wiki/Hygiene "Hygiene"), [preventive healthcare](/wiki/Preventive_healthcare "Preventive healthcare"), and [family planning](/wiki/Family_planning "Family planning") and treated common [illnesses](/wiki/Disease "Disease").[[52]](#cite_note-:16-52) Immunizations were provided free of charge.[[51]](#cite_note-:0222-51): 9 Public healthcare was highly effective in curbing infectious diseases in rural China.[[51]](#cite_note-:0222-51): 9 For treatment of major diseases, rural people traveled to state-owned hospitals.[[51]](#cite_note-:0222-51): 270

## 1968: Purges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: 1968: Purges")]

See also: [Cleansing the Class Ranks](/wiki/Cleansing_the_Class_Ranks "Cleansing the Class Ranks")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/SZ_%E6%B7%B1%E5%9C%B3%E5%8D%9A%E7%89%A9%E9%A4%A8_Shenzhen_Museum_%E6%B7%B1%E5%9C%B3%E6%94%B9%E9%9D%A9%E9%96%8B%E6%94%BE%E5%89%8D%E6%AD%B7%E5%8F%B2%E5%B1%95%E5%BB%B3_Before_Reform_and_Opening-up_History_IX1_Anti-Liu_Shaoqi.jpg/500px-SZ_%E6%B7%B1%E5%9C%B3%E5%8D%9A%E7%89%A9%E9%A4%A8_Shenzhen_Museum_%E6%B7%B1%E5%9C%B3%E6%94%B9%E9%9D%A9%E9%96%8B%E6%94%BE%E5%89%8D%E6%AD%B7%E5%8F%B2%E5%B1%95%E5%BB%B3_Before_Reform_and_Opening-up_History_IX1_Anti-Liu_Shaoqi.jpg)](/wiki/File:SZ_%E6%B7%B1%E5%9C%B3%E5%8D%9A%E7%89%A9%E9%A4%A8_Shenzhen_Museum_%E6%B7%B1%E5%9C%B3%E6%94%B9%E9%9D%A9%E9%96%8B%E6%94%BE%E5%89%8D%E6%AD%B7%E5%8F%B2%E5%B1%95%E5%BB%B3_Before_Reform_and_Opening-up_History_IX1_Anti-Liu_Shaoqi.jpg)

A rally in opposition to [Liu Shaoqi](/wiki/Liu_Shaoqi "Liu Shaoqi")

In May 1968, Mao launched a massive political purge. Many people were sent to the countryside to work in reeducation camps. Generally, the campaign targeted rebels from the CR's earlier, more populist, phase.[[42]](#cite_note-Xu2022-42): 239 On 27 July, the Red Guards' power over the PLA was officially ended, and the establishment sent in units to besiege areas that remained untouched by the Guards. A year later, the Red Guard factions were dismantled entirely; Mao predicted that the chaos might begin running its own agenda and be tempted to turn against revolutionary ideology. Their purpose had been largely fulfilled; Mao and his radical colleagues had largely overturned established power.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Liu was expelled from the CCP at the 12th Plenum of the [8th Central Committee](/wiki/8th_Central_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "8th Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party") in September, and labelled the "headquarters of the bourgeoisie".[[53]](#cite_note-53)

### Mao meets with Red Guard leaders (July)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Mao meets with Red Guard leaders (July)")]

As the Red Guard movement had waned over the preceding year, violence by the remaining Red Guards increased on some Beijing campuses. Violence was particularly pronounced at [Tsinghua University](/wiki/Tsinghua_University "Tsinghua University"), where a few thousand hardliners of two factions continued to fight. At Mao's initiative, on 27 July 1968, tens of thousands of workers entered the Tsinghua campus shouting slogans in opposition to the violence. Red Guards attacked the workers, who remained peaceful. Ultimately, the workers disarmed the students and occupied the campus.[[54]](#cite_note-Russo2020-54): 205–206

On 28 July, Mao and the Central Group met with the five most important remaining Beijing Red Guard leaders to address the movement's excessive violence and political exhaustion.[[54]](#cite_note-Russo2020-54): 205–206 It was the only time during the Cultural Revolution that Mao met and addressed the student leaders directly. In response to a Red Guard leader's telegram sent prior to the meeting, which claimed that some "Black Hand" had maneuvered the workers against the Red Guards, Mao told the student leaders, "The Black Hand is nobody else but me! ... I asked [the workers] how to solve the armed fighting in the universities, and told them to go there to have a look."[[54]](#cite_note-Russo2020-54): 210

During the meeting, Mao and the Central Group for the Cultural Revolution stated, "[W]e want cultural struggle, we do not want armed struggle" and "The masses do not want civil war."[[54]](#cite_note-Russo2020-54): 217

> You have been involved in the Cultural Revolution for two years: struggle-criticism-transformation. Now, first, you're not struggling; second, you're not criticizing; and third, you're not transforming. Or rather, you are struggling, but it's an armed struggle. The people are not happy, the workers are not happy, city residents are not happy, most people in schools are not happy, most of the students even in your schools are not happy. Even within the faction that supports you, there are unhappy people. Is this the way to unify the world?

### Mao's cult of personality and "mango fever" (August)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Mao's cult of personality and \"mango fever\" (August)")]

Main article: [Mango cult](/wiki/Mango_cult "Mango cult")

See also: [Mao Zedong's cult of personality](/wiki/Mao_Zedong%27s_cult_of_personality "Mao Zedong's cult of personality") and [Mao Zedong Thought Propaganda Teams](/wiki/Mao_Zedong_Thought_Propaganda_Teams "Mao Zedong Thought Propaganda Teams")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/1967-11_1967%E5%B9%B4_%E6%AF%9B%E6%B3%BD%E4%B8%9C%E6%8E%A5%E8%A7%81%E7%BA%A2%E5%8D%AB%E5%85%B5%E6%B2%B9%E7%94%BB.jpg/250px-1967-11_1967%E5%B9%B4_%E6%AF%9B%E6%B3%BD%E4%B8%9C%E6%8E%A5%E8%A7%81%E7%BA%A2%E5%8D%AB%E5%85%B5%E6%B2%B9%E7%94%BB.jpg)](/wiki/File:1967-11_1967%E5%B9%B4_%E6%AF%9B%E6%B3%BD%E4%B8%9C%E6%8E%A5%E8%A7%81%E7%BA%A2%E5%8D%AB%E5%85%B5%E6%B2%B9%E7%94%BB.jpg)

A propaganda oil painting of Mao during the Cultural Revolution (1967)

In the spring of 1968, a massive campaign aimed at enhancing Mao's reputation began. On 4 August, Mao was presented with mangoes by the Pakistani foreign minister [Syed Sharifuddin Pirzada](/wiki/Sharifuddin_Pirzada "Sharifuddin Pirzada"), in an apparent diplomatic gesture.[[55]](#cite_note-Murck2013-55) Mao had his aide send the box of mangoes to his [propaganda team](/wiki/Mao_Zedong_Thought_Propaganda_Teams "Mao Zedong Thought Propaganda Teams") at [Tsinghua University](/wiki/Tsinghua_University "Tsinghua University") on 5 August, who were stationed there to quiet strife among Red Guard factions.[[56]](#cite_note-Walder2015-56)[[55]](#cite_note-Murck2013-55)

Several months of "mango fever" followed as the fruit became a focus of a "boundless loyalty" campaign for Mao. More replica mangoes were created, and the replicas were sent on tour around Beijing and elsewhere. Many revolutionary committees visited the mangoes in Beijing from outlying provinces. Approximately half a million people greeted the replicas when they arrived in [Chengdu](/wiki/Chengdu "Chengdu"). Badges and wall posters featuring the mangoes and Mao were produced in the millions.[[56]](#cite_note-Walder2015-56)

The fruit was shared among all institutions that had been a part of the propaganda team, and large processions were organized in support of the "precious gift", as the mangoes were known.[[57]](#cite_note-Leese2011-57)

It has been claimed that Mao used the mangoes to express support for the workers who would go to whatever lengths necessary to end the factional fighting among students, and a "prime example of Mao's strategy of symbolic support."[[58]](#cite_note-Schrift2001-58) Through early 1969, participants of Mao Zedong Thought study classes in Beijing returned with mass-produced mango facsimiles, gaining media attention in the provinces.[[57]](#cite_note-Leese2011-57)

### Down to the Countryside Movement (December)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Down to the Countryside Movement (December)")]

Main article: [Down to the Countryside Movement](/wiki/Down_to_the_Countryside_Movement "Down to the Countryside Movement")

In December 1968, Mao began the Down to the Countryside Movement. During this movement, which lasted for the following decade, young bourgeoisie living in cities were ordered to go to the countryside to experience working life. The term "young intellectuals" was used to refer to recent college graduates. In the late 1970s, these students returned to their home cities. Many students who were previously Red Guard supported the movement and Mao's vision. This movement was thus in part a means of moving Red Guards from the cities to the countryside, where they would cause less social disruption. It also served to spread revolutionary ideology geographically.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

## 1969–1971: Lin Biao

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: 1969–1971: Lin Biao")]

The [9th National Congress](/wiki/9th_National_Congress_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "9th National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party") was held in April 1969. It served as a means to "revitalize" the party with fresh thinking—as well as new cadres, after much of the old guard had been destroyed in the struggles of the preceding years.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 285 The party framework established two decades earlier broke down almost entirely: rather than through an election by party members, delegates for this Congress were effectively selected by Revolutionary Committees.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 288 Representation of the military increased by a large margin from the previous Congress, reflected in the election of more PLA members to the new Central Committee—over 28%. Many officers now elevated to senior positions were loyal to PLA Marshal Lin Biao, which would open a new rift between the military and civilian leadership.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 292

> We do not only feel boundless joy because we have as our great leader the greatest Marxist–Leninist of our era, Chairman Mao, but also great joy because we have Vice Chairman Lin as Chairman Mao's universally recognized successor.

— Premier Zhou Enlai at the 9th Party Congress[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 291

Reflecting this, Lin was officially elevated to become the Party's preeminent figure outside of Mao, with his name written into the [party constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Constitution of the Chinese Communist Party") as his "closest comrade-in-arms" and "universally recognized successor".[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 291 At the time, no other Communist parties or governments anywhere in the world had adopted the practice of enshrining a successor to the current leader into their constitutions. Lin delivered the keynote address at the Congress: a document drafted by hardliner leftists Yao Wenyuan and Zhang Chunqiao under Mao's guidance.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 289

The report was heavily critical of Liu Shaoqi and other "counter-revolutionaries" and drew extensively from quotations in the *Little Red Book*. The Congress solidified the central role of Maoism within the party, re-introducing Maoism as the official guiding ideology in the party constitution. The Congress elected a new Politburo with Mao, Lin, Chen, Zhou Enlai and Kang as the members of the new Politburo Standing Committee.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 290

Lin, Chen, and Kang were all beneficiaries of the Cultural Revolution. Zhou, who was demoted in rank, voiced his unequivocal support for Lin at the Congress.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 290 Mao restored the function of some formal party institutions, such as the operations of the Politburo, which ceased functioning between 1966 and 1968 because the CCRG held de facto control.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 296

In early 1970, the nationwide "[One Strike-Three Anti Campaign](/wiki/One_Strike-Three_Anti_Campaign "One Strike-Three Anti Campaign")" was launched by Mao and the Communist Party Central, aiming to consolidate the new organs of power by targeting counterrevolutionary thoughts and actions.[[1]](#cite_note-Song-2011a-1) A large number of "minor criminals" were executed or forced to commit suicide between 1970 and 1972.[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61) According to government statistics released after the Cultural Revolution, during the campaign 1.87 million people were persecuted as traitors, spies, and counterrevolutionaries, and over 284,800 were arrested or killed from February to November 1970 alone.[[1]](#cite_note-Song-2011a-1)

### PLA encroachment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: PLA encroachment")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Mao_Zedong_and_Lin_Biao_during_the_1967_May_Day_parade.jpg/500px-Mao_Zedong_and_Lin_Biao_during_the_1967_May_Day_parade.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mao_Zedong_and_Lin_Biao_during_the_1967_May_Day_parade.jpg)

Mao (left) and Lin (right) in 1967, riding in the back of a vehicle during an [International Workers' Day](/wiki/International_Workers%27_Day "International Workers' Day") parade

Mao's efforts at re-organizing party and state institutions generated mixed results. The situation in some of the provinces remained volatile, even as the political situation in Beijing stabilized. Factional struggles, many violent, continued at a local level despite the declaration that the 9th National Congress marked a temporary victory for the CR.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 316 Furthermore, despite Mao's efforts to put on a show of unity at the Congress, the factional divide between Lin's PLA camp and the Jiang-led radical camp was intensifying. Indeed, a personal dislike of Jiang drew many civilian leaders, including Chen, closer to Lin.[[62]](#cite_note-Jin-62): 115

Between 1966 and 1968, China was isolated internationally, having declared its enmity towards both the USSR and the US. The friction with the USSR intensified after [border clashes](/wiki/Sino-Soviet_border_conflict "Sino-Soviet border conflict") on the [Ussuri River](/wiki/Ussuri_River "Ussuri River") in March 1969 as Chinese leaders prepared for all-out war.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 317 In June 1969, the PLA's enforcement of political discipline and suppression of the factions that had emerged during the Cultural Revolution became intertwined with the central Party's efforts to accelerate [Third Front](/wiki/Third_Front_(China) "Third Front (China)"). Those who did not return to work would be viewed as engaging in 'schismatic activity' which risked undermining preparations to defend China from potential invasion.[[15]](#cite_note-Meyskens2020-15): 150–151

In October 1969, the Party attempted to focus more on war preparedness and less on suppressing factions.[[15]](#cite_note-Meyskens2020-15): 151 That month, senior leaders were evacuated from Beijing. Amid the tension, Lin issued the "[Order Number One](/wiki/Order_Number_One_(Lin_Biao) "Order Number One (Lin Biao)")", which appeared to be an executive order to prepare for war to the PLA's eleven [military regions](/wiki/Military_regions_of_the_People%27s_Liberation_Army "Military regions of the People's Liberation Army") on October 18 without going through Mao. This drew the ire of the chairman, who saw it as evidence that his declared successor was usurping his authority.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 317

The prospect of war elevated the PLA to greater prominence in domestic politics, increasing Lin's stature at Mao's expense.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 321 Some evidence suggests that Mao was pushed to seek closer relations with the US as a means to avoid PLA dominance that would result from a military confrontation with the Soviet Union.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 321 During his later meeting with [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") in 1972, Mao hinted that Lin had opposed better relations with the U.S.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 322

#### Restoration of State Chairman position

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Restoration of State Chairman position")]

Main article: [Abolition of the presidency in China](/wiki/Abolition_of_the_presidency_in_China "Abolition of the presidency in China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/%E5%89%8D%E4%B8%AD%E5%85%B1%E5%9C%8B%E5%AE%B6%E4%B8%BB%E5%B8%AD%E5%8A%89%E5%B0%91%E5%A5%87%E5%9C%A8%E6%96%87%E9%9D%A9%E4%B8%AD%E8%A2%AB%E9%AC%A5%E7%88%AD%EF%BC%8C%E4%B8%A6%E9%81%AD%E8%BB%9F%E7%A6%81%E6%8A%98%E7%A3%A8%EF%BC%8C%E7%B5%82%E6%96%BC%E7%97%85%E9%80%9D%E6%B2%B3%E5%8D%97%EF%BC%8C%E5%85%B6%E9%81%BA%E9%AB%94%E8%A2%AB%E7%A7%98%E5%AF%86%E7%81%AB%E5%8C%96.jpg/250px-%E5%89%8D%E4%B8%AD%E5%85%B1%E5%9C%8B%E5%AE%B6%E4%B8%BB%E5%B8%AD%E5%8A%89%E5%B0%91%E5%A5%87%E5%9C%A8%E6%96%87%E9%9D%A9%E4%B8%AD%E8%A2%AB%E9%AC%A5%E7%88%AD%EF%BC%8C%E4%B8%A6%E9%81%AD%E8%BB%9F%E7%A6%81%E6%8A%98%E7%A3%A8%EF%BC%8C%E7%B5%82%E6%96%BC%E7%97%85%E9%80%9D%E6%B2%B3%E5%8D%97%EF%BC%8C%E5%85%B6%E9%81%BA%E9%AB%94%E8%A2%AB%E7%A7%98%E5%AF%86%E7%81%AB%E5%8C%96.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E5%89%8D%E4%B8%AD%E5%85%B1%E5%9C%8B%E5%AE%B6%E4%B8%BB%E5%B8%AD%E5%8A%89%E5%B0%91%E5%A5%87%E5%9C%A8%E6%96%87%E9%9D%A9%E4%B8%AD%E8%A2%AB%E9%AC%A5%E7%88%AD%EF%BC%8C%E4%B8%A6%E9%81%AD%E8%BB%9F%E7%A6%81%E6%8A%98%E7%A3%A8%EF%BC%8C%E7%B5%82%E6%96%BC%E7%97%85%E9%80%9D%E6%B2%B3%E5%8D%97%EF%BC%8C%E5%85%B6%E9%81%BA%E9%AB%94%E8%A2%AB%E7%A7%98%E5%AF%86%E7%81%AB%E5%8C%96.jpg)

PRC Chairman (President) Liu Shaoqi on his deathbed in 1969

After Lin was confirmed as Mao's successor, his supporters focused on the restoration of the position of State Chairman,[[note 1]](#cite_note-63) which had been abolished by Mao after Liu's purge. They hoped that by allowing Lin to ease into a constitutionally sanctioned role, whether Chairman or vice-chairman, Lin's succession would be institutionalized.
