The consensus within the [Politburo](/wiki/Politburo_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Politburo of the Chinese Communist Party") was that Mao should assume the office with Lin as vice-chairman; but perhaps wary of Lin's ambitions or for other unknown reasons, Mao voiced his explicit opposition.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 327

Factional rivalries intensified at the Second Plenum of the Ninth Congress in Lushan held in late August 1970. Chen, now aligned with the PLA faction loyal to Lin, galvanized support for the restoration of the office of President of China, despite Mao's wishes. Moreover, Chen launched an assault on Zhang, a staunch Maoist who embodied the chaos of the Cultural Revolution, over the evaluation of Mao's legacy.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 328–331

The attacks on Zhang found favour with many Plenum attendees and may have been construed by Mao as an indirect attack on the CR. Mao confronted Chen openly, denouncing him as a "false Marxist",[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 332 and removed him from the Politburo Standing Committee. In addition to the purge of Chen, Mao asked Lin's principal generals to write self-criticisms on their political positions as a warning to Lin. Mao also inducted several of his supporters to the Central Military Commission and placed loyalists in leadership roles of the [Beijing Military Region](/wiki/Beijing_Military_Region "Beijing Military Region").[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 332

### Project 571

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Project 571")]

Main article: [Project 571](/wiki/Project_571 "Project 571")

By 1971, the diverging interests of the civilian and military leaders were apparent. Mao was troubled by the PLA's newfound prominence, and the purge of Chen marked the beginning of a gradual scaling-down of the PLA's political involvement.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 353 According to official sources, sensing the reduction of Lin's power base and his declining health, Lin's supporters plotted to use the military power still at their disposal to oust Mao in a coup.[[62]](#cite_note-Jin-62)

Lin's son [Lin Liguo](/wiki/Lin_Liguo "Lin Liguo"), along with other high-ranking military conspirators, formed a coup apparatus in Shanghai and dubbed the plan to oust Mao *Outline for Project 571* – in the original Mandarin, the phrase sounds similar to the term for 'military uprising'. It is disputed whether Lin Biao was directly involved in this process. While official sources maintain that Lin did plan and execute the coup attempt, scholars such as Jin Qiu portray Lin as passive, cajoled by elements among his family and supporters. Qiu contests that Lin Biao was ever personally involved in drafting the *Outline*, with evidence suggesting that Lin Liguo was directly responsible for the draft.[[62]](#cite_note-Jin-62)

#### Lin's flight and plane crash

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Lin's flight and plane crash")]

Main article: [Lin Biao incident](/wiki/Lin_Biao_incident "Lin Biao incident")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Huayangpalace_layers_2007_09.jpg/500px-Huayangpalace_layers_2007_09.jpg)](/wiki/File:Huayangpalace_layers_2007_09.jpg)

Graffiti of Lin Biao's foreword to the *[Little Red Book](/wiki/Little_Red_Book "Little Red Book")*, with his name (lower right) later scratched out

According to the official CCP narrative, on 13 September, Lin Biao, his wife [Ye Qun](/wiki/Ye_Qun "Ye Qun"), Lin Liguo, and members of his staff attempted to flee to the USSR ostensibly to seek political asylum. En route, Lin's plane crashed in Mongolia, killing all on board. The plane apparently ran out of fuel. A Soviet investigative team was not able to determine the cause of the crash but hypothesized that the pilot was flying low to evade radar and misjudged the plane's altitude.

The account was questioned by those who raised doubts over Lin's choice of the USSR as a destination, the plane's route, the identity of the passengers, and whether or not a coup was actually taking place.[[62]](#cite_note-Jin-62)[[63]](#cite_note-64)

On 13 September, the Politburo met in an emergency session to discuss Lin. His death was confirmed in Beijing only on 30 September, which led to the cancellation of the [National Day](/wiki/National_Day_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "National Day of the People's Republic of China") celebration events the following day. The Central Committee did not release news of Lin's death to the public until two months later. Many Lin supporters sought refuge in Hong Kong. Those who remained on the mainland were purged.[[62]](#cite_note-Jin-62)

The event caught the party leadership off guard: the concept that Lin could betray Mao de-legitimized a vast body of Cultural Revolution political rhetoric and by extension, Mao's absolute authority. For several months following the incident, the party information apparatus struggled to find a "correct way" to frame the incident for public consumption, but as the details came to light, the majority of the Chinese public felt disillusioned and realised they had been manipulated for political purposes.[[62]](#cite_note-Jin-62)

## 1972–1976: The Gang of Four

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: 1972–1976: The Gang of Four")]

Main article: [Gang of Four](/wiki/Gang_of_Four "Gang of Four")

Mao became depressed and reclusive after the Lin incident. Sensing a sudden loss of direction, Mao reached out to old comrades whom he had denounced in the past. Meanwhile, in September 1972, Mao transferred a 38-year-old cadre from Shanghai, Wang Hongwen, to Beijing and made him Party vice-chairman. Wang, a former factory worker from a peasant background,[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 357 was seemingly getting groomed for succession.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 364

Jiang's position strengthened after Lin's flight. She held tremendous influence with the radical camp. With Mao's health on the decline, Jiang's political ambitions began to emerge. She allied herself with Wang and propaganda specialists Zhang Chunqiao and Yao Wenyuan, forming a political clique later pejoratively dubbed as the [Gang of Four](/wiki/Gang_of_Four "Gang of Four").[[64]](#cite_note-Yao_Wenyuan-65)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/1967-03_1966%E5%B9%B412%E6%9C%8826%E6%97%A5%E6%B1%9F%E9%9D%92%E5%91%A8%E6%81%A9%E6%9D%A5%E5%BA%B7%E7%94%9F%E6%8E%A5%E8%A7%81%E7%BA%A2%E5%8D%AB%E5%85%B5.jpg/250px-1967-03_1966%E5%B9%B412%E6%9C%8826%E6%97%A5%E6%B1%9F%E9%9D%92%E5%91%A8%E6%81%A9%E6%9D%A5%E5%BA%B7%E7%94%9F%E6%8E%A5%E8%A7%81%E7%BA%A2%E5%8D%AB%E5%85%B5.jpg)](/wiki/File:1967-03_1966%E5%B9%B412%E6%9C%8826%E6%97%A5%E6%B1%9F%E9%9D%92%E5%91%A8%E6%81%A9%E6%9D%A5%E5%BA%B7%E7%94%9F%E6%8E%A5%E8%A7%81%E7%BA%A2%E5%8D%AB%E5%85%B5.jpg)

[Jiang Qing](/wiki/Jiang_Qing "Jiang Qing") (left) receiving Red Guards in Beijing with [Zhou Enlai](/wiki/Zhou_Enlai "Zhou Enlai") (center) and [Kang Sheng](/wiki/Kang_Sheng "Kang Sheng"), with each holding a copy of the *Little Red Book*

By 1973, round after round of political struggles had left many lower-level institutions, including local government, factories, and railways, short of competent staff to carry out basic functions.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 340 China's economy had fallen into disarray, which led to the rehabilitation of purged lower-level officials. The party's core became heavily dominated by Cultural Revolution beneficiaries and radicals, whose focus remained ideological purity over economic productivity. The economy remained mostly Zhou's domain, one of the few remaining moderates. Zhou attempted to restore the economy, but was resented by the Gang of Four, who identified him as their primary political succession threat.[[65]](#cite_note-66)

In late 1973, to weaken Zhou's political position and to distance themselves from Lin's apparent betrayal, the [Criticize Lin, Criticize Confucius](/wiki/Criticize_Lin,_Criticize_Confucius "Criticize Lin, Criticize Confucius") campaign began under Jiang's leadership.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 366 Its stated goals were to purge China of [New Confucianist](/wiki/New_Confucianism "New Confucianism") thinking and denounce Lin's actions as traitorous and regressive.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 372

### Deng Xiaoping's rehabilitation (1975)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Deng Xiaoping's rehabilitation (1975)")]

Deng Xiaoping returned to the political scene, assuming the post of Vice-Premier in March 1973, in the first of a series of Mao-approved promotions. After Zhou withdrew from active politics in January 1975, Deng was effectively put in charge of the government, party, and military, then adding the additional titles of [PLA General Chief of Staff](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army_General_Staff_Department "People's Liberation Army General Staff Department"), [Vice Chairman of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Vice_Chairman_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Vice Chairman of the Chinese Communist Party"), and vice-chairman of the Central Military Commission.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 381

Mao wanted to use Deng as a counterweight to the military faction in government to suppress former Lin loyalists. In addition, Mao had also lost confidence in the Gang of Four and saw Deng as the alternative. Leaving the country in grinding poverty would damage the positive legacy of the CR, which Mao worked hard to protect. Deng's return set the scene for a protracted factional struggle between the radical Gang of Four and moderates led by Zhou and Deng.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

At the time, Jiang and associates held effective control of mass media and the party's [propaganda network](/wiki/Propaganda_in_China "Propaganda in China"), while Zhou and Deng held control of most government organs. On some decisions, Mao sought to mitigate the Gang's influence, but on others, he acquiesced to their demands. The Gang of Four's political and media control did not prevent Deng from enacting his economic policies. Deng emphatically opposed Party factionalism, and his policies aimed to promote unity to restore economic productivity. Much like the post-Great Leap restructuring led by Liu Shaoqi, Deng streamlined the [railway system](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_China "Rail transport in China"), [steel production](/wiki/Steel_industry_in_China "Steel industry in China"), etc. By late 1975, however, Mao saw that Deng's economic restructuring might negate the CR's legacy and launched the [Counterattack the Right-Deviationist Reversal-of-Verdicts Trend](/wiki/Counterattack_the_Right-Deviationist_Reversal-of-Verdicts_Trend "Counterattack the Right-Deviationist Reversal-of-Verdicts Trend"), a campaign to oppose "rehabilitating the case for the rightists", alluding to Deng as the country's foremost "rightist". Mao directed Deng to write self-criticisms in November 1975, a move lauded by the Gang of Four.[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 381

### Death of Zhou Enlai

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Death of Zhou Enlai")]

On 8 January 1976, Zhou Enlai died of bladder cancer. On 15 January, Deng delivered Zhou's eulogy in a funeral attended by all of China's most senior leaders with the notable absence of Mao, who had grown increasingly critical of Zhou.[[66]](#cite_note-Teiwes-67): 217–218[[67]](#cite_note-Spence-68): 610 After Zhou's death, Mao selected the relatively unknown [Hua Guofeng](/wiki/Hua_Guofeng "Hua Guofeng") instead of a member of the Gang of Four or Deng to become Premier.[[68]](#cite_note-69)

The Gang of Four grew apprehensive that spontaneous, large-scale popular support for Zhou could turn the political tide against them. They acted through the media to impose restrictions on public displays of mourning for Zhou. Years of resentment over the CR, the public persecution of Deng—seen as Zhou's ally—and the prohibition against public mourning led to a rise in popular discontent against Mao and the Gang of Four. Official attempts to enforce the mourning restrictions included removing public memorials and tearing down posters commemorating Zhou's achievements. On 25 March 1976, Shanghai's *[Wen Hui Bao](/wiki/Wen_Hui_Bao "Wen Hui Bao")* published an article calling Zhou "the [capitalist roader](/wiki/Capitalist_roader "Capitalist roader") inside the Party [who] wanted to help the unrepentant capitalist roader [Deng] regain his power." These propaganda efforts at smearing Zhou's image, however, only strengthened public attachment to Zhou's memory.[[66]](#cite_note-Teiwes-67): 213–214

### Tiananmen incident

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Tiananmen incident")]

Main article: [1976 Tiananmen incident](/wiki/1976_Tiananmen_incident "1976 Tiananmen incident")

On 4 April 1976, on the eve of China's annual [Qingming Festival](/wiki/Qingming_Festival "Qingming Festival"), a traditional day of mourning, thousands of people gathered around the [Monument to the People's Heroes](/wiki/Monument_to_the_People%27s_Heroes "Monument to the People's Heroes") in Tiananmen Square to commemorate Zhou. They honored Zhou by laying wreaths, banners, poems, placards, and flowers at the foot of the Monument.[[67]](#cite_note-Spence-68): 612 The most apparent purpose of this memorial was to eulogize Zhou, but the Gang of Four were also attacked for their actions against the Premier. A small number of slogans left at Tiananmen even attacked Mao and his Cultural Revolution.[[66]](#cite_note-Teiwes-67): 218

Up to two million people may have visited Tiananmen Square on 4 April. All levels of society, from the most impoverished peasants to high-ranking PLA officers and the children of high-ranking cadres, were represented in the activities. Those who participated were motivated by a mixture of anger over Zhou's treatment, revolt against the Cultural Revolution and apprehension for China's future. The event did not appear to have coordinated leadership.[[66]](#cite_note-Teiwes-67): 218–220

The Central Committee, under the leadership of Jiang Qing, labelled the event 'counter-revolutionary' and cleared the square of memorial items shortly after midnight on April 6. Attempts to suppress the mourners led to a riot. Police cars were set on fire, and a crowd of over 100,000 people forced its way into several government buildings surrounding the square. Many of those arrested were later sentenced to prison. Similar incidents occurred in other major cities. Jiang and her allies attacked Deng as the incident's 'mastermind', and issued reports on official media to that effect. Deng was formally stripped of all positions inside and outside the Party on 7 April. This marked Deng's second purge.[[67]](#cite_note-Spence-68): 612

### Death of Mao Zedong and the Gang of Four's downfall

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Death of Mao Zedong and the Gang of Four's downfall")]

See also: [Death and state funeral of Mao Zedong](/wiki/Death_and_state_funeral_of_Mao_Zedong "Death and state funeral of Mao Zedong") and [Smashing the Gang of Four](/wiki/Smashing_the_Gang_of_Four "Smashing the Gang of Four")

On 9 September 1976, Mao Zedong died. To Mao's supporters, his death symbolized the loss of China's revolutionary foundation. His death was announced on 9 September.[[69]](#cite_note-70) The nation descended into grief and mourning, with people weeping in the streets and public institutions closing for over a week. Hua Guofeng chaired the Funeral Committee and delivered the memorial speech.[[70]](#cite_note-71)[[71]](#cite_note-72)

Shortly before dying, Mao had allegedly written the message "[With you in charge, I'm at ease](/wiki/With_you_in_charge,_I%27m_at_ease "With you in charge, I'm at ease")," to Hua. Hua used this message to substantiate his position as successor. Hua had been widely considered to be lacking in political skill and ambitions, and seemingly posed no serious threat to the Gang of Four in the race for succession. However, the Gang's radical ideas also clashed with influential elders and many Party reformers. With army backing and the support of Marshal Ye Jianying, Director of Central Office [Wang Dongxing](/wiki/Wang_Dongxing "Wang Dongxing"), Vice Premier [Li Xiannian](/wiki/Li_Xiannian "Li Xiannian") and party elder [Chen Yun](/wiki/Chen_Yun "Chen Yun"), on 6 October, the [Central Security Bureau](/wiki/Central_Security_Bureau "Central Security Bureau")'s Special Unit 8341 had all members of the Gang of Four arrested in a bloodless coup.[[72]](#cite_note-73)

After Mao's death, people characterized as 'beating-smashing-looting elements', who were seen as having disturbed the social order during the CR, were purged or punished. "Beating-smashing-looting elements" had typically been aligned with rebel factions.[[42]](#cite_note-Xu2022-42): 359

## Aftermath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Aftermath")]

### Transitional period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Transitional period")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Hua_Guofeng-1.jpg/250px-Hua_Guofeng-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hua_Guofeng-1.jpg)

[Hua Guofeng](/wiki/Hua_Guofeng "Hua Guofeng") succeeded Mao as chairman of the CCP after Mao's death.

Although Hua denounced the Gang of Four in 1976, he continued to invoke Mao's name to justify Mao-era policies. Hua spearheaded what became known as the [Two Whatevers](/wiki/Two_Whatevers "Two Whatevers").[[73]](#cite_note-Harding-74) Like Deng, Hua wanted to reverse the CR's damage; but unlike Deng, who wanted new economic models for China, Hua intended to move the Chinese economic and political system towards [Soviet-style planning](/wiki/Soviet-type_economic_planning "Soviet-type economic planning").[[74]](#cite_note-75)[[75]](#cite_note-76)

On 10 October, Deng wrote a letter to Hua asking to be transferred back to state and party affairs; party elders also called for Deng's return. With increasing pressure from all sides, Premier Hua named Deng Vice-Premier in July 1977, and later promoted him to various other positions, effectively elevating Deng to be China's second-most powerful figure. In August, the [11th National Congress](/wiki/11th_National_Congress_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "11th National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party") was held in Beijing, officially naming (in ranking order) Hua Guofeng, Ye Jianying, Deng Xiaoping, [Li Xiannian](/wiki/Li_Xiannian "Li Xiannian") and Wang Dongxing as new members of the Politburo Standing Committee.[[76]](#cite_note-77)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

### Repudiation and reform under Deng

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Repudiation and reform under Deng")]

See also: [Boluan Fanzheng](/wiki/Boluan_Fanzheng "Boluan Fanzheng"), [1978 Truth Criterion Controversy](/wiki/1978_Truth_Criterion_Controversy "1978 Truth Criterion Controversy"), and [Reforms and Opening Up](/wiki/Reforms_and_Opening_Up "Reforms and Opening Up")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Deng_Xiaoping.jpg/250px-Deng_Xiaoping.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deng_Xiaoping.jpg)

[Deng Xiaoping](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping") became the [paramount leader of China](/wiki/Paramount_Leader_of_China "Paramount Leader of China") in 1978. He started the process of [reform and opening up](/wiki/Reforms_and_Opening_Up "Reforms and Opening Up")

Deng Xiaoping first proposed what he called *Boluan Fanzheng* in September 1977 in order to correct the mistakes of the Cultural Revolution.[[77]](#cite_note-78)[[78]](#cite_note-79) In May 1978, Deng seized the opportunity to elevate his protégé [Hu Yaobang](/wiki/Hu_Yaobang "Hu Yaobang") to power. Hu published an article in the *[Guangming Daily](/wiki/Guangming_Daily_(China) "Guangming Daily (China)")*, making clever use of Mao's quotations, while lauding Deng's ideas. Following this article, Hua began to shift his tone in support of Deng. On 1 July, Deng publicized Mao's self-criticism report of 1962 regarding the failure of the Great Leap Forward. As his power base expanded, in September Deng began openly attacking Hua Guofeng's "Two Whatevers".[[73]](#cite_note-Harding-74) The "[1978 Truth Criterion Discussion](/wiki/1978_Truth_Criterion_Controversy "1978 Truth Criterion Controversy")", launched by Deng and Hu and their allies, also triggered a decade-long [New Enlightenment](/wiki/New_Enlightenment_(China) "New Enlightenment (China)") movement in mainland China, promoting democracy, [humanism](/wiki/Humanism "Humanism") and universal values, while opposing the ideology of Cultural Revolution.[[79]](#cite_note-80)[[80]](#cite_note-81)

On 18 December 1978, [Third Plenum of the 11th Central Committee](/wiki/3rd_Plenary_Session_of_the_11th_Central_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "3rd Plenary Session of the 11th Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party") was held. Deng called for "a liberation of thoughts" and urged the party to "[seek truth from facts](/wiki/Seek_truth_from_facts "Seek truth from facts")" and abandon ideological dogma. The Plenum officially marked the beginning of the [economic reform era](/wiki/Economic_reform_in_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Economic reform in the People's Republic of China"). Hua Guofeng engaged in self-criticism and called his "Two Whatevers" a mistake. At the Plenum, the Party reversed its verdict on the Tiananmen Incident. Former Chinese president Liu Shaoqi was given a belated state funeral.[[81]](#cite_note-82) Peng Dehuai, who was persecuted to death during the Cultural Revolution was rehabilitated in 1978.

At the Fifth Plenum held in 1980, Peng Zhen, [He Long](/wiki/He_Long "He Long") and other leaders who had been purged during the Cultural Revolution were rehabilitated. Hu Yaobang became head of the [party secretariat](/wiki/Secretariat_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Secretariat of the Chinese Communist Party") as its [secretary-general](/wiki/Secretary-general_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Secretary-general of the Chinese Communist Party"). In September, Hua Guofeng resigned, and [Zhao Ziyang](/wiki/Zhao_Ziyang "Zhao Ziyang"), another Deng ally, was named [premier](/wiki/Premier_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Premier of the People's Republic of China"). Hua remained on the Central Military Commission, but formal power was transferred to a new generation of pragmatic reformers, who reversed Cultural Revolution policies to a large extent. Within a few years, Deng and Hu helped rehabilitate over 3 million "unjust, false, erroneous" cases.[[82]](#cite_note-83) In particular, the trial of the Gang of Four took place in Beijing from 1980 to 1981, and the court stated that 729,511 people had been persecuted by the Gang, of whom 34,800 were said to have died.[[83]](#cite_note-84)

In 1981, the Chinese Communist Party passed a resolution and declared that the Cultural Revolution was "responsible for the most severe setback and the heaviest losses suffered by the Party, the country, and the people since the founding of the People's Republic."[[84]](#cite_note-PRC-2020-85)[[85]](#cite_note-Wilson_Center-1981-86)[[86]](#cite_note-CCCCP-11-87)

## Atrocities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Atrocities")]

### Death toll

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Death toll")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Xi_Zhongxun_on_struggle_session_in_September_1967.jpg/250px-Xi_Zhongxun_on_struggle_session_in_September_1967.jpg)](/wiki/File:Xi_Zhongxun_on_struggle_session_in_September_1967.jpg)

A [struggle session](/wiki/Struggle_session "Struggle session") in September 1967 targeting [Xi Zhongxun](/wiki/Xi_Zhongxun "Xi Zhongxun"), the father of [Xi Jinping](/wiki/Xi_Jinping "Xi Jinping"), who had been labeled an "anti-party element"[[87]](#cite_note-88)[[88]](#cite_note-89)[[89]](#cite_note-90)

Fatality estimates vary across different sources, usually ranging from hundreds of thousands to millions.[[90]](#cite_note-91)[[91]](#cite_note-Song-2011c-92)[[92]](#cite_note-Ling-2011-93)[[93]](#cite_note-Strauss-1994-94) In addition to various regimes of secrecy and obfuscation concerning the Revolution, both top-down as perpetuated by authorities, as well as laterally among the Chinese public in the decades since, the discrepancies are due in large part to the totalistic nature of the Revolution itself: it is a significant challenge for historians to discern whether and in what ways discrete events that took place during the Cultural Revolution should be ascribed to it.[[94]](#cite_note-95)

Most deaths occurred after the mass movements ended,[[46]](#cite_note-Walder2019b-46): 23 when organized campaigns attempted to consolidate order in workplaces and communities.[[46]](#cite_note-Walder2019b-46): 172 As [Andrew G. Walder](/wiki/Andrew_G._Walder "Andrew G. Walder") summarizes, "The cure for factional warfare was far worse than the disease."[[46]](#cite_note-Walder2019b-46): 23 Serious man-made disasters such as the [1975 Banqiao Dam failure](/wiki/1975_Banqiao_Dam_failure "1975 Banqiao Dam failure"), also caused many deaths.

[Literature reviews](/wiki/Literature_review "Literature review") of the overall death toll due to the Cultural Revolution usually include the following:[[91]](#cite_note-Song-2011c-92)[[95]](#cite_note-Yan2016-96)[[96]](#cite_note-open2012-97)

| Time | Source | Deaths (in millions) | Remarks |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2014 | [Andrew G. Walder](/wiki/Andrew_G._Walder "Andrew G. Walder") | 1.1–1.6[[97]](#cite_note-Walder-2014-98) | Examines the period between 1966 and 1971.[[97]](#cite_note-Walder-2014-98) Walder reviewed the reported deaths in 2,213 annals from every [county](/wiki/County_(China) "County (China)") and interpreted the annals' vague language in the most conservative manner. For instance, "some died" and "a couple died" were interpreted as zero death, while "death in the scale of tens/hundreds/thousands" were interpreted as "ten/a hundred/a thousand died". The reported deaths underestimate the actual deaths, especially because some annals actively covered up deaths.[[91]](#cite_note-Song-2011c-92)[[96]](#cite_note-open2012-97)[[98]](#cite_note-Song-2017-99) Annal editors were supervised by the [CCP Propaganda Department](/wiki/Propaganda_Department_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Propaganda Department of the Chinese Communist Party").[[91]](#cite_note-Song-2011c-92)[[98]](#cite_note-Song-2017-99) In 2003, Walder and Yang Su coauthored a paper along this approach, but with fewer county annals available at the time.[[95]](#cite_note-Yan2016-96)[[99]](#cite_note-100) |
| 1999 | Ding Shu | 2[[100]](#cite_note-Open_Magazine-1999-101) | Ding's figures include 100,000 killed in the Red Terror during 1966, with 200,000 forced to commit suicide, plus 300,000–500,000 killed in violent struggles, 500,000 during [Cleansing the Class Ranks](/wiki/Cleansing_the_Class_Ranks "Cleansing the Class Ranks"), 200,000 during [One Strike-Three Anti Campaign](/wiki/One_Strike-Three_Anti_Campaign "One Strike-Three Anti Campaign") and the [Anti-May Sixteenth Elements Campaign](/wiki/May_Sixteenth_elements "May Sixteenth elements").[[100]](#cite_note-Open_Magazine-1999-101)[[91]](#cite_note-Song-2011c-92)[[95]](#cite_note-Yan2016-96)[[96]](#cite_note-open2012-97) |
| 1996 | CCP History Research Center | 1.728[[101]](#cite_note-ConsensusNet-2016-102) | The 1.728 million were counted as "[unnatural deaths](/wiki/Unnatural_death "Unnatural death")", among which 9.4% (162,000) were CCP party members and 252,000 were intellectuals. The figures were extracted from 建国以来历次政治运动事实; 'Facts on the Successive Political Movements since the Founding of the PRC', a book by the party's History Research Center, which states that "according to CCP internal investigations in 1978 and 1984 ... 21.44 million were investigated, 125 million got implicated in these investigations; [...] 4.2 million were detained (by Red Guards and other non-police), 1.3 million were arrested by police, 1.728 million of unnatural deaths; [...] 135,000 were executed for crimes of counter-revolution; [...] during *violent struggles* 237,000 were killed and 7.03 million became disabled".[[101]](#cite_note-ConsensusNet-2016-102)[[102]](#cite_note-103) While these internal investigations were never mentioned or published in any other official documents, the scholarly consensus found these figures very reasonable.[[96]](#cite_note-open2012-97)[[103]](#cite_note-Chen_1998-104) |
| 1991 | [Rudolph J. Rummel](/wiki/Rudolph_J._Rummel "Rudolph J. Rummel") | 7.731[[104]](#cite_note-Rummel-1991-105) | Rummel included his estimate of *[Laogai](/wiki/Laogai "Laogai")* camp deaths in this figure.[[95]](#cite_note-Yan2016-96) He estimated that 5% of the 10 million people in the Laogai camps died each year of the 12-year period, and that this amounts to roughly 6 million.[[104]](#cite_note-Rummel-1991-105) |
| 1982 | [Ye Jianying](/wiki/Ye_Jianying "Ye Jianying") | 3.42–20[[96]](#cite_note-open2012-97)[[105]](#cite_note-Pye-1986-106) | Several sources have quoted a statement made by [Marshal](/wiki/Yuanshuai#People's_Republic_of_China "Yuanshuai") [Ye Jianying](/wiki/Ye_Jianying "Ye Jianying"), of "683,000 deaths in the cities, 2.5 million deaths in the countryside, plus 123,700 deaths due to [violent struggles](/wiki/Violent_Struggle "Violent Struggle") and 115,500 deaths due to [struggle sessions](/wiki/Struggle_session "Struggle session") and imprisonment, in addition to 557,000 people missing."[[96]](#cite_note-open2012-97)[[101]](#cite_note-ConsensusNet-2016-102)[[106]](#cite_note-Dai-2016-107) In a 2012 interview with Hong Kong's *Open Magazine*, an unnamed bureaucrat in Beijing claimed that Ye made the statement in a 1982 CCP meeting, while he was the party's [Vice Chairman](/wiki/Vice_Chairman_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Vice Chairman of the Chinese Communist Party").[[96]](#cite_note-open2012-97)[[106]](#cite_note-Dai-2016-107) Several sources have also quoted that Marshal Ye estimated the death toll to be 20 million during a CCP working conference in December 1978.[[92]](#cite_note-Ling-2011-93)[[105]](#cite_note-Pye-1986-106)[[107]](#cite_note-WangS-2012-108)[[96]](#cite_note-open2012-97) Academic Jie Li writes that scholarship has discredited Ye's 20 million estimate.[[50]](#cite_note-:Li2-50): 340 |
| 1979 | *[Agence France Presse](/wiki/Agence_France_Presse "Agence France Presse")* | 0.4[[108]](#cite_note-AGP-1979-109) | This figure was obtained by an AFP correspondent in Beijing, citing an unnamed but "usually reliable" source.[[108]](#cite_note-AGP-1979-109) In 1986, [Maurice Meisner](/wiki/Maurice_Meisner "Maurice Meisner") referred to this number as a "widely accepted nationwide figure", but also said "The toll may well have been higher. It is unlikely that it was less."[[109]](#cite_note-110) Jonathan Leightner asserted that the number is "perhaps one of the best estimates".[[110]](#cite_note-111) |

### Massacres

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Massacres")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Quotations_from_Chairman_Mao_Zedong_in_Wuxuan_2.jpg/250px-Quotations_from_Chairman_Mao_Zedong_in_Wuxuan_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Quotations_from_Chairman_Mao_Zedong_in_Wuxuan_2.jpg)

Quotations of Mao Zedong on a street wall of [Wuxuan County](/wiki/Wuxuan_County "Wuxuan County"), one of the centers of the Guangxi Massacre

Massacres took place across China, including in [Guangxi](/wiki/Guangxi_Massacre "Guangxi Massacre"), [Inner Mongolia](/wiki/Inner_Mongolia_incident "Inner Mongolia incident"), [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong_Cultural_Revolution_Massacre "Guangdong Cultural Revolution Massacre"), [Yunnan](/wiki/Yunnan_Cultural_Revolution_Massacre_(disambiguation) "Yunnan Cultural Revolution Massacre (disambiguation)"), [Hunan](/wiki/Hunan_Cultural_Revolution_Massacre_(disambiguation) "Hunan Cultural Revolution Massacre (disambiguation)"), [Ruijin](/wiki/Ruijin_Massacre "Ruijin Massacre"), and [Qinghai](/wiki/Qinghai_223_Incident "Qinghai 223 Incident"), as well as [Red August](/wiki/Red_August "Red August") in Beijing.[[1]](#cite_note-Song-2011a-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Wang-2001-2)

These massacres were mainly led and organized by local revolutionary committees, Communist Party branches, militia, and the military.[[1]](#cite_note-Song-2011a-1)[[111]](#cite_note-Song-2002-112)[[112]](#cite_note-Yang-2006-113) Most victims were members of the [Five Black Categories](/wiki/Five_Black_Categories "Five Black Categories") as well as their children, or members of "rebel groups". Chinese scholars have estimated that at least 300,000 people died in these massacres.[[111]](#cite_note-Song-2002-112)[[113]](#cite_note-Yang-2017-114) Collective killings in [Guangxi](/wiki/Guangxi "Guangxi") and [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong") were among the most serious. In Guangxi, the official annals of at least 43 counties have records of massacres, with 15 of them reporting a death toll of over 1,000, while in Guangdong at least 28 county annals record massacres, with 6 of them reporting a death toll of over 1,000.[[112]](#cite_note-Yang-2006-113)

Official sources in 1980 revealed that, during the Red August, at least 1,772 people were killed by Red Guards, including teachers and principals of many schools, meanwhile 33,695 homes were ransacked and 85,196 families were forced to flee.[[2]](#cite_note-Wang-2001-2)[[114]](#cite_note-Jian-2015-115)[[115]](#cite_note-NPR-2014-116) The [Daxing Massacre](/wiki/Daxing_Massacre "Daxing Massacre") in rural Beijing caused the deaths of 325 people from 27 August to 1 September 1966; those killed ranged from 80 years old to a 38-day old baby, with 22 families being completely wiped out.[[1]](#cite_note-Song-2011a-1)[[114]](#cite_note-Jian-2015-115)[[116]](#cite_note-Yu-2020-117)

In [Dao County](/wiki/Dao_County "Dao County"), Hunan, a total of 7,696 people were killed [from 13 August to 17 October 1967](/wiki/Daoxian_massacre "Daoxian massacre"), in addition to 1,397 forced to commit suicide, and 2,146 becoming permanently disabled.[[117]](#cite_note-118)[[118]](#cite_note-119)

In the [Guangxi Massacre](/wiki/Guangxi_Massacre "Guangxi Massacre"), [the official record](/wiki/Secret_Archives_about_the_Cultural_Revolution_in_Guangxi "Secret Archives about the Cultural Revolution in Guangxi") shows an estimated death toll from 100,000 to 150,000 as well as [cannibalism](/wiki/Human_cannibalism "Human cannibalism") primarily between 1967 and 1968 in Guangxi,[[119]](#cite_note-RFA-2016-120)[[120]](#cite_note-Yan-121) where one of the worst violent struggles of the Revolution took place, before Zhou sent the PLA to intervene.[[121]](#cite_note-Chang-122): 545

In 1975, the PLA led [a massacre](/wiki/Shadian_incident "Shadian incident") in [Yunnan](/wiki/Yunnan "Yunnan") around the town of Shadian, targeting [Hui people](/wiki/Hui_people "Hui people"), resulting in the deaths of more than 1,600 civilians, including 300 children, and the destruction of 4,400 homes.[[1]](#cite_note-Song-2011a-1)[[122]](#cite_note-Zhou-1999-123)[[123]](#cite_note-Liang-2018-124)

### The "Stashed" History Recorded by Li Zhensheng

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: The \"Stashed\" History Recorded by Li Zhensheng")]

While official state media required photographers to capture revolutionary fervor, some photojournalists secretly documented the movement's 'dark side' at great personal risk. Li Zhensheng, a photographer for the Heilongjiang Daily, amassed nearly 100,000 negatives—including images of struggle sessions and executions—which he hid under the parquet floorboards of his home in Harbin for decades. These images, many of which were not developed until years later, now serve as a rare visual testament to the daily realities of the era outside of Beijing.

### Violent struggles, struggle sessions, and purges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Violent struggles, struggle sessions, and purges")]

Main articles: [Violent Struggle](/wiki/Violent_Struggle "Violent Struggle"), [Struggle session](/wiki/Struggle_session "Struggle session"), and [Cleansing the Class Ranks](/wiki/Cleansing_the_Class_Ranks "Cleansing the Class Ranks")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/%E6%96%87%E9%9D%A9%E5%A2%93%E7%BE%A4.jpg/250px-%E6%96%87%E9%9D%A9%E5%A2%93%E7%BE%A4.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E6%96%87%E9%9D%A9%E5%A2%93%E7%BE%A4.jpg)

The Cultural Revolution Cemetery in [Chongqing](/wiki/Chongqing "Chongqing"), where 400–500 people killed in factional clashes are buried, out of a total of at least 1,700 deaths.[[124]](#cite_note-Buckley-125)

*Violent struggles* were factional conflicts (mostly among Red Guards and "rebel groups") that began in Shanghai and then spread to other areas in 1967. They brought the country to a state of civil war.[[1]](#cite_note-Song-2011a-1)[[125]](#cite_note-126) Weapons used included some 18.77 million guns[[note 2]](#cite_note-127), 2.72 million grenades, 14,828 cannons, millions of other ammunition and even armored cars and tanks.[[1]](#cite_note-Song-2011a-1) Notable violent struggles include the battles in Chongqing, in [Sichuan](/wiki/Sichuan "Sichuan"), and in [Xuzhou](/wiki/Xuzhou "Xuzhou").[[1]](#cite_note-Song-2011a-1)[[124]](#cite_note-Buckley-125)[[126]](#cite_note-128) Researchers claimed that the nationwide death toll in violent struggles ranged from 300,000 to 500,000.[[91]](#cite_note-Song-2011c-92)[[100]](#cite_note-Open_Magazine-1999-101)[[1]](#cite_note-Song-2011a-1)

The recorded rate of violence rose in 1967, reaching a peak that summer before dropping suddenly.[[46]](#cite_note-Walder2019b-46): 143 During 1967, casualties were relatively low as the weapons used were primarily clubs, spears, and rocks until late July.[[46]](#cite_note-Walder2019b-46): 143 Although firearms and heavier weapons began to spread during summer, most were neither trained nor committed fighters and therefore casualties remained relatively low.[[46]](#cite_note-Walder2019b-46): 143 The peak of collective violence in summer 1967 dropped sharply after August, when Mao became concerned about rebel attacks on local army units and thereafter made clear that his prior calls to "drag out" army commanders was a mistake and he would instead support besieged army commands.[[46]](#cite_note-Walder2019b-46): 150

The greatest number of casualties occurred during the process of restoring order in 1968, although the overall number of violent conflicts was lower. Walder stated that while "rising casualties from a smaller number of insurgent conflicts surely reflected the increasing scale and organizational coherence of rebel factions, and their growing access to military weaponry[,]" another important factor was that "[t]he longer that local factional warfare continued without the prospect of an equitable political settlement, the greater the stakes for the participants and the more intense the collective violence as factions fought to avoid the consequence of losing."[[46]](#cite_note-Walder2019b-46): 154–155

In addition to violent struggles, millions of Chinese were violently persecuted, especially via struggle sessions. Those identified as spies, "[running dogs](/wiki/Running_dog "Running dog")", "revisionists", or coming from a suspect class (including those related to former landlords or rich peasants) were subject to beating, imprisonment, rape, torture, sustained and systematic harassment and abuse, seizure of property, denial of medical attention, and erasure of social identity.[[73]](#cite_note-Harding-74) Some people were not able to stand the torture and committed suicide. Researchers claimed that at least 100,000 to 200,000 people committed suicide during the early CR.[[91]](#cite_note-Song-2011c-92)[[100]](#cite_note-Open_Magazine-1999-101)

At the same time, many "unjust, false, and mistaken" cases appeared due to political purges. In addition to those who died in massacres, a large number of people died or became permanently disabled due to [lynching](/wiki/Lynching "Lynching") or other forms of persecution. From 1968 to 1969, the Cleansing the Class Ranks purge caused the deaths of at least 500,000 people.[[1]](#cite_note-Song-2011a-1)[[127]](#cite_note-129) Purges of similar nature such as the [One Strike-Three Anti Campaign](/wiki/One_Strike-Three_Anti_Campaign "One Strike-Three Anti Campaign") and the campaign towards the [May Sixteenth elements](/wiki/May_Sixteenth_elements "May Sixteenth elements") were launched in the 1970s.[[128]](#cite_note-Song-2011b-130)[[100]](#cite_note-Open_Magazine-1999-101) For example, a political purge in Yunnan province, the [Zhao Jianmin spy case](/wiki/Zhao_Jianmin_spy_case "Zhao Jianmin spy case"), resulted in 17,000 deaths and wrongfully persecuted a total of 1.38 million people.[[1]](#cite_note-Song-2011a-1)

### Repression of ethnic minorities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Repression of ethnic minorities")]

See also: [Inner Mongolia incident](/wiki/Inner_Mongolia_incident "Inner Mongolia incident") and [Shadian incident](/wiki/Shadian_incident "Shadian incident")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/03/Panchen_Lama_during_the_struggle_%28thamzing%29_session_1964.jpg)](/wiki/File:Panchen_Lama_during_the_struggle_(thamzing)_session_1964.jpg)

The [Panchen Lama](/wiki/Panchen_Lama "Panchen Lama") during a struggle session

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Sampho_and_his_wife_during_the_Cultural_Revolution.jpg/250px-Sampho_and_his_wife_during_the_Cultural_Revolution.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sampho_and_his_wife_during_the_Cultural_Revolution.jpg)

Struggle session of [Sampho Tsewang Rigzin](/wiki/Sampho_Tsewang_Rigzin "Sampho Tsewang Rigzin") and his wife

The Cultural Revolution wrought havoc on minority cultures and ethnicities. Languages and customs of [ethnic minorities in China](/wiki/Ethnic_minorities_in_China "Ethnic minorities in China") were labeled as part of the Four Olds, texts in ethnic languages were burned, and bilingual education was suppressed.[[129]](#cite_note-131)[[130]](#cite_note-132)[[131]](#cite_note-133) In [Inner Mongolia](/wiki/Inner_Mongolia "Inner Mongolia"), some 790,000 people were persecuted during the Inner Mongolia incident. Of these, 22,900 were beaten to death, and 120,000 were maimed,[[13]](#cite_note-Mac-13): 258 during a witch hunt to find members of the alleged separatist New Inner Mongolian People's Revolutionary Party. In [Xinjiang](/wiki/Xinjiang "Xinjiang"), copies of the [Qur'an](/wiki/Qur%27an "Qur'an") and other books of the [Uyghur people](/wiki/Uyghur_people "Uyghur people") were apparently burned. Muslim imams reportedly were paraded around with paint splashed on their bodies.[[132]](#cite_note-Yongming_Zhou_1999,_p._162-134)

In [Yunnan](/wiki/Yunnan "Yunnan") Province, the palace of the [Dai people](/wiki/Dai_people "Dai people")'s king was torched, and a massacre of Muslim [Hui people](/wiki/Hui_people "Hui people") at the hands of the PLA in Yunnan, known as the [Shadian incident](/wiki/Shadian_incident "Shadian incident"), reportedly claimed over 1,600 lives in 1975.[[132]](#cite_note-Yongming_Zhou_1999,_p._162-134) After the Cultural Revolution, the government gave reparations for the Shadian Incident, including the erection of a Martyr's Memorial in Shadian.[[133]](#cite_note-135)

In the [ethnic Korean](/wiki/Koreans_in_China "Koreans in China") areas of northeast China, clashes took place. In [Yanbian Korean Autonomous Prefecture](/wiki/Yanbian_Korean_Autonomous_Prefecture "Yanbian Korean Autonomous Prefecture"), where freight trains trundled from China into North Korea, corpses of Koreans killed in the pitched battles of the Cultural Revolution were draped with revolutionary graffiti. The Governor, [Zhu Dehai](/wiki/Zhu_Dehai "Zhu Dehai"), an ethnic Korean, was branded as a traitor and a North Korean spy and was later purged.[[134]](#cite_note-136)

Concessions to minorities were abolished during the Cultural Revolution as part of the Red Guards' attack on the "Four Olds". [People's communes](/wiki/People%27s_commune "People's commune"), previously only established in parts of Tibet, were established throughout [Tibet Autonomous Region](/wiki/Tibet_Autonomous_Region "Tibet Autonomous Region") in 1966,[[135]](#cite_note-137) removing Tibet's exemption from China's land reform, and reimposed in other minority areas. The effect on Tibet was particularly severe as it came following the repression after the [1959 Tibetan uprising](/wiki/1959_Tibetan_uprising "1959 Tibetan uprising").[[136]](#cite_note-138)[[137]](#cite_note-cycle-139) The destruction of nearly all of its over 6,000 monasteries, which began before the Cultural Revolution, were often conducted with the complicity of local ethnic Tibetan Red Guards.[[138]](#cite_note-Ardley-140): 9 Only eight were intact by the end of the 1970s.[[139]](#cite_note-141)

Many monks and nuns were killed, and the general population was subjected to physical and psychological torture.[[138]](#cite_note-Ardley-140): 9 An estimated 600,000 monks and nuns lived in Tibet in 1950, but by 1979, most were dead, imprisoned or had disappeared.[[138]](#cite_note-Ardley-140): 22 The Tibetan government in exile claimed that many Tibetans died from famines in 1961–1964 and 1968–1973 as a result of forced collectivization,[[137]](#cite_note-cycle-139)[[140]](#cite_note-142) however, the number of Tibetan deaths or whether famines, in fact, took place in these periods is disputed.[[141]](#cite_note-143)[[142]](#cite_note-144)[[143]](#cite_note-145) Despite persecution, some local leaders and minority ethnic practices survived in remote regions.[[144]](#cite_note-146)

It was felt that pushing minority groups too hard would compromise China's border defenses. This was especially important as minorities make up a large percentage of the population that live in border regions. In the late 1960s, China experienced a period of strained relations with some of its neighbors, notably with the Soviet Union and India.[[145]](#cite_note-Dreyer-147)

### Rape and sexual abuse

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Rape and sexual abuse")]

Further information: [Sent-down youth § Sexual violence](/wiki/Sent-down_youth#Sexual_violence "Sent-down youth")

[Pan Suiming](/wiki/Pan_Suiming "Pan Suiming"), Emily Honig, and others documented that rape and sexual abuse of sent-down women were common during the Cultural Revolution's height.[[146]](#cite_note-148)[[147]](#cite_note-149) Tania Branigan documented that women raped tended to be from educated urban backgrounds while their rapists were poor peasants or local officials.[[148]](#cite_note-150)[[149]](#cite_note-151)

## Cultural impact and influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Cultural impact and influence")]

### Red Guards riot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Red Guards riot")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Beijing_1968_I.jpg/250px-Beijing_1968_I.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beijing_1968_I.jpg)

A 1968 map of Beijing showing streets and landmarks renamed during the Cultural Revolution. Andingmen Inner Street became "Great Leap Forward Road", Taijichang Street became the "Road for Eternal Revolution", Dongjiaominxiang was renamed "Anti-Imperialist Road", Beihai Park was renamed "Worker-Peasant-Soldier Park" and Jingshan Park became "Red Guard Park". Most of the Cultural Revolution-era name changes were later reversed.

The revolution aimed to destroy the Four Olds and establish the corresponding Four News, which ranged from changing of names and cutting of hair to ransacking homes, vandalizing cultural treasures, and desecrating temples.[[23]](#cite_note-Lu-23): 61–64

The revolution aimed to eliminate [cow demons and snake spirits](/wiki/Cow_demons_and_snake_spirits "Cow demons and snake spirits") - the class enemies who promoted bourgeois ideas, as well as those from an exploitative family background or who belonged to one of the Five Black Categories. Large numbers of people perceived to be "monsters and demons" regardless of guilt or innocence were publicly denounced, humiliated, and beaten. In their revolutionary fervor, students, especially the Red Guards, denounced their teachers, and children denounced their parents.[[23]](#cite_note-Lu-23): 59–61 Many died from ill-treatment or committed suicide. In 1968, youths were mobilized to go to the countryside in the [Down to the Countryside Movement](/wiki/Down_to_the_Countryside_Movement "Down to the Countryside Movement") so they may learn from the peasantry, and the departure of millions from the cities helped end the most violent phase of the Cultural Revolution.[[150]](#cite_note-King-152): 176

### Academics and intellectuals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Academics and intellectuals")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Yao_Tongbin.jpg/250px-Yao_Tongbin.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yao_Tongbin.jpg)

[Yao Tongbin](/wiki/Yao_Tongbin "Yao Tongbin"), one of China's foremost [missile](/wiki/Missile "Missile") scientists, was beaten to death by a mob in Beijing during the Cultural Revolution (1968). This caused [Zhou Enlai](/wiki/Zhou_Enlai "Zhou Enlai") to order special protection for key technical experts.[[151]](#cite_note-153)

Academics and [intellectuals](/wiki/Intellectual "Intellectual") were regarded as the "[Stinking Old Ninth](/wiki/Stinking_Old_Ninth "Stinking Old Ninth")" and were widely persecuted. Many were sent to rural labor camps such as the [May Seventh Cadre School](/wiki/May_Seventh_Cadre_School "May Seventh Cadre School"). The prosecution of the Gang of Four revealed that 142,000 cadres and teachers in the education circles were persecuted. Academics, scientists, and educators who died included [Xiong Qinglai](/wiki/Xiong_Qinglai "Xiong Qinglai"), [Jian Bozan](/wiki/Jian_Bozan "Jian Bozan"), Wu Han, [Rao Yutai](/wiki/Rao_Yutai "Rao Yutai"), [Wu Dingliang](/wiki/Wu_Dingliang "Wu Dingliang"), [Yao Tongbin](/wiki/Yao_Tongbin "Yao Tongbin") and [Zhao Jiuzhang](/wiki/Zhao_Jiuzhang "Zhao Jiuzhang").[[152]](#cite_note-documents-154) As of 1968, among the 171 senior members who worked at the headquarters of [Chinese Academy of Sciences](/wiki/Chinese_Academy_of_Sciences "Chinese Academy of Sciences") in Beijing, 131 were persecuted. Among the members of the academy, 229 died.[[153]](#cite_note-155) As of September 1971, more than 4,000 staff members of China's nuclear center in [Qinghai](/wiki/Qinghai "Qinghai") had been persecuted, while more than 310 were disabled, over 40 committed suicide, and 5 were [executed](/wiki/Capital_punishment "Capital punishment").[[154]](#cite_note-156)[[155]](#cite_note-157)

Despite the hardships, some significant achievements came in science and technology: scientists tested the first missile, created China's first [hydrogen bomb](/wiki/Hydrogen_bomb "Hydrogen bomb") and launched China's first satellite in the "[Two Bombs, One Satellite](/wiki/Two_Bombs,_One_Satellite "Two Bombs, One Satellite")" program.[[156]](#cite_note-158)[[157]](#cite_note-159)

Many health personnel were deployed to the countryside as [barefoot doctors](/wiki/Barefoot_doctors "Barefoot doctors"). Some farmers were given informal medical training, and health-care centers were established in rural communities. This process led to a marked improvement in health and life expectancy.[[158]](#cite_note-Huang_Foreign_Affairs_2011-160)

### Education system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Education system")]

In the early months of Cultural Revolution, schools and universities were closed. Secondary school classes of 1966, 1967, and 1968 were unable to graduate on time later and became known as the "Old Three Cohort (老三届)".[[42]](#cite_note-Xu2022-42): 362 Colleges and universities were closed until 1970, and most universities did not reopen until 1972.[[159]](#cite_note-Joel-161): 164 [University entrance exams](/wiki/National_College_Entrance_Examination "National College Entrance Examination") were cancelled after 1966 (until the beginning of *[Boluan Fanzheng](/wiki/Boluan_Fanzheng "Boluan Fanzheng")* period in 1977), replaced by a system whereby students were recommended by factories, villages and military units. Traditional values were abandoned.[[23]](#cite_note-Lu-23): 195

On the other hand, industrial universities were established in factories to supply technical and engineering programs for industrial workers, inspired by Mao's July 1968 remarks advocating [vocational education](/wiki/Vocational_education "Vocational education").[[42]](#cite_note-Xu2022-42): 362 Factories around the country therefore established their own educational programs for technicians and engineers, and by 1976, there were 15,000 such 21 July Universities.[[160]](#cite_note-:Minami-162): 92 [Gao Mobo](/wiki/Gao_Mobo "Gao Mobo") observes that in many underprivileged areas, political campaigns brought improvements in education and public health.[[49]](#cite_note-:023-49): 119–120

In the initial stage of the [Down to the Countryside Movement](/wiki/Down_to_the_Countryside_Movement "Down to the Countryside Movement"), most of the youth who took part volunteered. Later on, the government forced them to move. Between 1968 and 1979, 17 million urban youth left for the countryside. Living in the rural areas deprived them of higher education.[[150]](#cite_note-King-152): 10 This generation is sometimes referred to as the "lost generation".[[161]](#cite_note-163) In the post-Mao period, many of those forcibly moved attacked the policy as a violation of their human rights.[[162]](#cite_note-Gao-164): 36 Formal literacy measurements did not resume until the 1980s.[[163]](#cite_note-Peterson-165) Some counties in [Zhanjiang](/wiki/Zhanjiang "Zhanjiang") had literacy rates as low as 59% 20 years after the revolution. This was amplified by the elimination of qualified teachers—many districts were forced to rely on students to teach.[[163]](#cite_note-Peterson-165)

Primary and middle schools gradually reopened during the Cultural Revolution. Schooling years were reduced and education standards fell, but the proportion of Chinese children who completed primary education increased from less than half to almost all, and the fraction who completed junior middle school rose from 15% to over two-thirds. Educational opportunities for rural children expanded, while education of the urban elite were restricted by anti-elitist policies.[[159]](#cite_note-Joel-161): 166–167 Radical policies provided many in rural communities with middle school education for the first time.[[159]](#cite_note-Joel-161): 163 Rural infrastructure developed during this period, facilitated by the political changes that empowered ordinary rural people.[[164]](#cite_note-Han2008-166): 177

### Slogans and rhetoric

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Slogans and rhetoric")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/1967-04_1967%E5%B9%B4%E9%9D%A9%E5%91%BD%E6%97%A0%E7%BD%AA%E9%80%A0%E5%8F%8D%E6%9C%89%E7%90%86.jpg/250px-1967-04_1967%E5%B9%B4%E9%9D%A9%E5%91%BD%E6%97%A0%E7%BD%AA%E9%80%A0%E5%8F%8D%E6%9C%89%E7%90%86.jpg)](/wiki/File:1967-04_1967%E5%B9%B4%E9%9D%A9%E5%91%BD%E6%97%A0%E7%BD%AA%E9%80%A0%E5%8F%8D%E6%9C%89%E7%90%86.jpg)

A Red Guard holding up the *[Selected Works of Mao Zedong](/wiki/Selected_Works_of_Mao_Zedong "Selected Works of Mao Zedong")*, with "revolution is no crime, to rebel is justified" written on a flag next to him, 1967

Huang claimed that the Cultural Revolution had massive effects on Chinese society because of the extensive use of political slogans.[[165]](#cite_note-huang-167) He claimed that slogans played a central role in rallying Party leadership and citizens. For example, the slogan "to rebel is justified" (造反有理; *zàofǎn yǒulǐ*) affected many views.[[165]](#cite_note-huang-167)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Cultural_revolution_anhui.jpg/250px-Cultural_revolution_anhui.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cultural_revolution_anhui.jpg)

The remnants of a banner containing slogans from the Cultural Revolution in [Anhui](/wiki/Anhui "Anhui"), 2006

Huang asserted that slogans were ubiquitous in people's lives, printed onto everyday items such as bus tickets, cigarette packets, and mirror tables.[[162]](#cite_note-Gao-164): 14 Workers were supposed to "grasp revolution and promote production".[[165]](#cite_note-huang-167)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

Political slogans had three sources: Mao, Party media such as *People's Daily*, and the Red Guards.[[165]](#cite_note-huang-167) Mao often offered vague, yet powerful directives that divided the Red Guards.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChangHalliday2005-168) These directives could be interpreted to suit personal interests, in turn aiding factions' goals in claiming loyalty to Mao.[[165]](#cite_note-huang-167)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

Dittmer and Chen claim that the [Chinese language](/wiki/Chinese_language_in_the_United_States "Chinese language in the United States") had historically been defined by subtlety, delicacy, moderation, and honesty, as well as the cultivation of a "refined and elegant literary style".[[167]](#cite_note-Dittmer-169) This changed during the CR. These slogans were an effective method of "thought reform", mobilizing millions in a concerted attack upon the subjective world, "while at the same time reforming their objective world."[[165]](#cite_note-huang-167)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[167]](#cite_note-Dittmer-169): 12

Dittmer and Chen argued that the emphasis on politics made language into effective propaganda, but "also transformed it into a jargon of stereotypes—pompous, repetitive, and boring".[[167]](#cite_note-Dittmer-169): 12 To distance itself from the era, Deng's government cut back on political slogans. During a eulogy for Deng's death, CCP general secretary [Jiang Zemin](/wiki/Jiang_Zemin "Jiang Zemin") called the Cultural Revolution a "grave mistake".[[168]](#cite_note-170)

### Arts and literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Arts and literature")]

In 1966, [Jiang Qing](/wiki/Jiang_Qing "Jiang Qing") advanced the *Theory of the Dictatorship of the Black Line*. Those perceived to be bourgeois, anti-socialist or anti-Mao (black line) should be cast aside, and called for the creation of new literature and arts.[[44]](#cite_note-Jiaqi-44): 352–353 Disseminators of the "old culture" would be eradicated. The majority of writers and artists were seen as "black line figures" and "reactionary literati", and were persecuted, and subjected to "criticism and denunciation" where they could be humiliated and ravaged, and be imprisoned or sent to hard labour.[[169]](#cite_note-Hong-171): 213–214 For instance, [Mei Zhi](/wiki/Mei_Zhi "Mei Zhi") and her husband were sent to a tea farm in [Lushan County, Sichuan](/wiki/Lushan_County,_Sichuan "Lushan County, Sichuan"). She did not resume writing until the 1980s.[[170]](#cite_note-sina-172)

In 1970, the CCP came to view the [Ministry of Culture](/wiki/Ministry_of_Culture_(China) "Ministry of Culture (China)") as so disruptive that it decided to dissolve the Ministry and establish a Culture Group within the [State Council](/wiki/State_Council_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "State Council of the People's Republic of China") in an effort to rein in cultural politics.[[160]](#cite_note-:Minami-162): 160 The principles for cultural production laid out by Mao in the 1942 [Talks at the Yan'an Forum on Art and Literature](/wiki/Yan%27an_Forum "Yan'an Forum") became dogmatized.[[171]](#cite_note-173) The literary situation eased after 1972, as more were allowed to write, and many provincial literary periodicals resumed publication, but the majority of writers still could not work.[[169]](#cite_note-Hong-171): 219–20 Documents released in 1980 regarding the prosecution of the Gang of Four show that more than 2,600 people in the field of arts and literature were persecuted by the Ministry of Culture.[[152]](#cite_note-documents-154) Many died: the names of 200 writers and artists who were persecuted to death were commemorated in 1979. These include writers such as [Lao She](/wiki/Lao_She "Lao She"), [Fu Lei](/wiki/Fu_Lei "Fu Lei"), [Deng Tuo](/wiki/Deng_Tuo "Deng Tuo"), [Baren](/wiki/Baren_(author) "Baren (author)"), [Li Guangtian](/wiki/Li_Guangtian "Li Guangtian"), [Yang Shuo](/wiki/Yang_Shuo_(writer) "Yang Shuo (writer)") and [Zhao Shuli](/wiki/Zhao_Shuli "Zhao Shuli").[[169]](#cite_note-Hong-171): 213–14

Depictions of [iron girls](/wiki/Iron_Girls "Iron Girls") became a frequent subject of art during the Cultural Revolution, often shown in spaces and activities traditionally associated with male authority as part of an effort to develop the [new socialist woman](/wiki/New_Man_(utopian_concept) "New Man (utopian concept)").[[172]](#cite_note-:Evans2-174): 100

#### Opera and music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Opera and music")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Revolutionary_opera.jpg/500px-Revolutionary_opera.jpg)](/wiki/File:Revolutionary_opera.jpg)

The ballet *[The Red Detachment of Women](/wiki/Red_Detachment_of_Women_(ballet) "Red Detachment of Women (ballet)")*, one of the Model Dramas promoted during the Cultural Revolution

Jiang took control of the stage and introduced [revolutionary operas](/wiki/Revolutionary_opera "Revolutionary opera") under her direct supervision. Traditional operas were banned as they were considered feudalistic and bourgeois, but revolutionary opera, which modified [Peking opera](/wiki/Peking_opera "Peking opera") in both content and form, was promoted.[[23]](#cite_note-Lu-23): 115 Six operas and two ballets were produced in the first three years, most notably the opera *[The Legend of the Red Lantern](/wiki/The_Legend_of_the_Red_Lantern "The Legend of the Red Lantern")*. These operas were the only approved opera form. Other opera troupes were required to adopt or change their repertoire.[[150]](#cite_note-King-152): 176 [Loyalty dances](/wiki/Loyalty_dance "Loyalty dance") became common and were performed throughout the country by both professional cultural workers and ordinary people.[[42]](#cite_note-Xu2022-42): 362 The model operas were broadcast on the radio, made into films, blared from public loudspeakers, taught to students in schools and workers in factories, and became ubiquitous as a form of popular entertainment and were the only theatrical entertainment for millions.[[44]](#cite_note-Jiaqi-44): 352–53[[23]](#cite_note-Lu-23): 115 Most model dramas featured women as their leads and promoted Chinese state feminism.[[173]](#cite_note-Karl-2010-175) Their narratives begin with them oppressed by [misogyny](/wiki/Misogyny "Misogyny"), class position, and imperialism before liberating themselves through the discovery of internal strength and the CCP.[[173]](#cite_note-Karl-2010-175)

During the Cultural Revolution, composers of *[Yellow Music](/wiki/Yellow_Music "Yellow Music")*, which had already been banned following the communist revolution, were persecuted, including [Li Jinhui](/wiki/Li_Jinhui "Li Jinhui") who was killed in 1967.[[174]](#cite_note-176) Revolution-themed songs instead were promoted, and songs such as "[Ode to the Motherland](/wiki/Ode_to_the_Motherland "Ode to the Motherland")", "[Sailing the Seas Depends on the Helmsman](/wiki/Sailing_the_Seas_Depends_on_the_Helmsman "Sailing the Seas Depends on the Helmsman")", "[The East Is Red](/wiki/The_East_Is_Red_(song) "The East Is Red (song)")" and "[Without the Communist Party, There Would Be No New China](/wiki/Without_the_Communist_Party,_There_Would_Be_No_New_China "Without the Communist Party, There Would Be No New China")" were either written or became popular during this period. "The East Is Red", especially, became popular; it *de facto* supplanted "[March of the Volunteers](/wiki/March_of_the_Volunteers "March of the Volunteers")" (lyrics author [Tian Han](/wiki/Tian_Han "Tian Han") persecuted to death) as the national anthem of China, though the latter was later restored to its previous place.[[175]](#cite_note-:2-177)

"Quotation songs", in which Mao's quotations were set to music, were particularly popular during the early years of the Cultural Revolution.[[176]](#cite_note-Coderre2021-178): 34 Composer Li Jiefu first published quotation songs in *People's Daily* in September 1966 and they were promoted thereafter as a means for studying *Quotations from Chairman Mao Zedong*.[[177]](#cite_note-:2323-179): 47 Records of quotation songs were played over loudspeakers, their primary means of distribution,[[176]](#cite_note-Coderre2021-178): 35 as the use of transistor radios lagged until 1976.[[176]](#cite_note-Coderre2021-178): 32–33 [Rusticated youths](/wiki/Rusticated_youths "Rusticated youths") with an interest in broadcast technology frequently operated rural radio stations after 1968.[[176]](#cite_note-Coderre2021-178): 42 At the 9th National Congress of the Communist Party, Jiang Qing condemned quotation songs, which she had come to view as comparable to yellow music.[[177]](#cite_note-:2323-179): 43

#### Visual arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Visual arts")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/SZ_%E6%B7%B1%E5%9C%B3%E5%8D%9A%E7%89%A9%E9%A4%A8_Shenzhen_Museum_%E6%B7%B1%E5%9C%B3%E6%94%B9%E9%9D%A9%E9%96%8B%E6%94%BE%E5%89%8D%E6%AD%B7%E5%8F%B2%E5%B1%95%E5%BB%B3_Before_Reform_and_Opening-up_History_Exhibition_Hall_%E6%96%87%E9%9D%A9%E6%99%82%E4%BB%A3%E7%B4%85%E8%89%B2%E6%80%9D%E6%83%B3%E5%AE%A3%E5%82%B3%E6%B5%B7%E5%B8%83_political_posters_IX1_03.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:SZ_%E6%B7%B1%E5%9C%B3%E5%8D%9A%E7%89%A9%E9%A4%A8_Shenzhen_Museum_%E6%B7%B1%E5%9C%B3%E6%94%B9%E9%9D%A9%E9%96%8B%E6%94%BE%E5%89%8D%E6%AD%B7%E5%8F%B2%E5%B1%95%E5%BB%B3_Before_Reform_and_Opening-up_History_Exhibition_Hall_%E6%96%87%E9%9D%A9%E6%99%82%E4%BB%A3%E7%B4%85%E8%89%B2%E6%80%9D%E6%83%B3%E5%AE%A3%E5%82%B3%E6%B5%B7%E5%B8%83_political_posters_IX1_03.jpg)

Posters from the Cultural Revolution period

Aesthetic principles emphasized during the Cultural Revolution included the "tall, big, complete," "red, bright, shining," and "the three prominences".[[178]](#cite_note-:22-180): 187–188 According to the principle of the "three prominences," the good are more prominent than the bad, the very good are more prominent than the good, and the one outstanding figure is more prominent than the very good.[[179]](#cite_note-:0-181): 4 Other stylistic principles of the Cultural Revolution included "tall, large, and full".[[172]](#cite_note-:Evans2-174): 96

Among the most significant visual works of the Cultural Revolution was Liu Chunhua's 1967 oil painting, *Chairman Mao Goes to Anyuan*.[[180]](#cite_note-:Tang-182): 132 Another influential painting was Pan Jiajun's 1972 *I Am a Petrel*, which depicts a young woman soldier repairing a telegraph cables during a storm.[[181]](#cite_note-:Evans-183): 94–95 Praised as a new classic of depicting a revolutionary heroine, it inspired the creation of similar works and was itself widely distributed as a poster.[[181]](#cite_note-:Evans-183): 95

A Red Guard art movement developed, reaching its peak in 1967.[[180]](#cite_note-:Tang-182): 128 Red Guards from fine arts academies organized large art exhibitions, often in cooperation with rebel groups in work units or the army, which included many amateur art works.[[180]](#cite_note-:Tang-182): 132 The most significant Red Guard art exhibition was *Long Live the Triumph of Chairman Mao's Revolutionary Line* which opened 1 October 1967 in Beijing and featured more than 1,600 art works in a variety of media produced by artists and amateurs from around the country.[[180]](#cite_note-:Tang-182): 132 Following the exhibition, traveling teams toured art works from the exhibition through rural China and into remote areas.[[180]](#cite_note-:Tang-182): 132 The Red Guard art movement favored forms of art deemed public or anti-elitist, such as black and white woodcuts (or brush and marker illustrations in the style of a woodcut), satirical cartoons, paper cut outs, and forms of [folk art](/wiki/Folk_art "Folk art").[[180]](#cite_note-:Tang-182): 132–133 Among the most popular motifs in Red Guard art was the image of a worker, peasant, and soldier conducting criticism or Red Guards doing so.[[180]](#cite_note-:Tang-182): 173 Red Guard groups in fine arts academies also published journals, pamphlets, and manifestos through which they criticized the old art institutions.[[180]](#cite_note-:Tang-182): 131

Traditional themes were sidelined and artists such as [Feng Zikai](/wiki/Feng_Zikai "Feng Zikai"), [Shi Lu](/wiki/Shi_Lu "Shi Lu"), and [Pan Tianshou](/wiki/Pan_Tianshou "Pan Tianshou") were persecuted.[[150]](#cite_note-King-152): 97 Many of the artists were assigned to manual labour, and artists were expected to depict subjects that glorified the Cultural Revolution related to their labour.[[182]](#cite_note-Andrews-184): 351–52 In 1971, in part to alleviate their suffering, several leading artists were recalled from manual labour or freed from captivity under a Zhou initiative to decorate hotels and railway stations defaced by Red Guard slogans. Zhou said that the artworks were meant for foreigners, therefore were "outer" art and not under the obligations and restrictions placed on "inner" art meant for Chinese citizens. He claimed that landscape paintings should not be considered one of the "Four Olds". However, Zhou was weakened by cancer, and in 1974, the Jiang faction seized these and other paintings and mounted exhibitions in Beijing, Shanghai and other cities denouncing the artworks as [Black Paintings](/wiki/Black_Painting_incident "Black Painting incident").[[182]](#cite_note-Andrews-184): 368–376

Propaganda in posters was used as a mass communication device and often served as the people's leading source of information. They were produced in large numbers and widely disseminated to propagate ideological positions.[[179]](#cite_note-:0-181): 4–5 The two main posters genres were the big-character poster or *[dazibao](/wiki/Dazibao "Dazibao")* and commercial propaganda poster.[[183]](#cite_note-Cushing-185): 7–12

* The *dazibao* presented slogans, poems, commentary and graphics often posted on walls in public spaces, factories and communes. Mao wrote his own *dazibao* at Beijing University on 5 August 1966, calling on the people to "Bombard the Headquarters".[[183]](#cite_note-Cushing-185): 5
* *[Xuanchuanhua](/wiki/Xuanchuan "Xuanchuan"),* or propaganda paintings, were artworks produced by the government and sold cheaply in stores to be displayed in homes or workplaces. The artists for these posters might be amateurs or uncredited professionals, and the posters were largely in a [Socialist Realist](/wiki/Socialist_Realist "Socialist Realist") visual style with specific conventions—for example, images of Mao were to be depicted as "red, smooth, and luminescent".[[183]](#cite_note-Cushing-185): 7–12[[182]](#cite_note-Andrews-184): 360

After decreasing in prominence throughout the 1980s, Cultural Revolution posters became prominent in public life again in the 1990s in connection with [red tourism](/wiki/Red_tourism "Red tourism"), as collectibles, in commercial advertising, and in contemporary art.[[184]](#cite_note-186): 9 In contemporary China, they continue to be reproduced in large amounts and sold commercially.[[181]](#cite_note-:Evans-183): 87 Historic posters have been the subject of exhibitions and auctions, including in the United States and Europe.[[181]](#cite_note-:Evans-183): 92

Before the Cultural Revolution, relatively few cultural productions reflected the lives of peasants and workers; during it, the struggles of workers, peasants, and revolutionary soldiers became frequent artistic subjects, often created by peasants and workers themselves.[[185]](#cite_note-Ching-2021-187) Among the most prominent examples of this style included the peasant paintings of Huxian.[[180]](#cite_note-:Tang-182): 133 In the early 1970s, worker, peasant, and soldier-created art was promoted as the paradigm of socialist art.[[180]](#cite_note-:Tang-182): 133 The spread of peasant paintings in rural China, for example, became one of the [newborn things](/wiki/Newborn_socialist_things "Newborn socialist things") celebrated in a socialist society.[[185]](#cite_note-Ching-2021-187)

#### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Literature")]

[Literature](/wiki/Chinese_literature "Chinese literature") adapted the aesthetic themes from the model works, such as the "three prominences".[[186]](#cite_note-:Kindler2-188): 179 Applied in the literary context, the principle of the three prominences was that texts should demonstrate the struggle between revolutionary and reactionary forces in a stark and [dichotomous](/wiki/Dichotomy "Dichotomy") manner.[[186]](#cite_note-:Kindler2-188): 179

During the Cultural Revolution, the long-form novel was an emphasized form of literature.[[186]](#cite_note-:Kindler2-188): 179 Among the major genres were novels about the experiences of sent-down youth.[[186]](#cite_note-:Kindler2-188): 179 These included novels written by sent-down youths themselves, such as [Zhang Kangkang](/wiki/Zhang_Kangkang "Zhang Kangkang")'s 1975 novel *Dividing Line* and Zhang Changgong's 1973 novel *Youth*.[[186]](#cite_note-:Kindler2-188): 179

Among the amateur writers who became prominent during the Cultural Revolution was [Duan Ruixia](/w/index.php?title=Duan_Ruixia&action=edit&redlink=1 "Duan Ruixia (page does not exist)") for the 1973 short story, *Not Just One of the Audience*.[[186]](#cite_note-:Kindler2-188): 187

Writing about the romantic relationships of revolutionary martyrs became one of the taboo topics during the Cultural Revolution.[[178]](#cite_note-:22-180): 186–187

#### Film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Film")]

The *Four Hundred Films to be Criticized* booklet was distributed, and film directors and actors/actresses were criticized with some tortured and imprisoned.[[44]](#cite_note-Jiaqi-44): 401–02 These included many of Jiang Qing's rivals and former friends. Those who died in the period included [Cai Chusheng](/wiki/Cai_Chusheng "Cai Chusheng"), [Zheng Junli](/wiki/Zheng_Junli "Zheng Junli"), [Shangguan Yunzhu](/wiki/Shangguan_Yunzhu "Shangguan Yunzhu"), [Wang Ying](/wiki/Wang_Ying_(actress) "Wang Ying (actress)"), and [Xu Lai](/wiki/Xu_Lai_(actress) "Xu Lai (actress)").[[187]](#cite_note-189) No feature films were produced in mainland China for seven years apart from a few approved "Model dramas" and highly ideological films.[[188]](#cite_note-190) A notable example is *[Taking Tiger Mountain by Strategy](/wiki/Taking_Tiger_Mountain_by_Strategy_(film) "Taking Tiger Mountain by Strategy (film)")*.[[189]](#cite_note-191)[[190]](#cite_note-192) China rejected Hollywood films and most foreign films.[[27]](#cite_note-Li2023-27): 213 [Albanian films](/wiki/Cinema_of_Albania "Cinema of Albania") and [North Korean films](/wiki/Cinema_of_North_Korea "Cinema of North Korea") developed mass audiences in China.[[27]](#cite_note-Li2023-27): 213 In 1972, Chinese officials invited [Michelangelo Antonioni](/wiki/Michelangelo_Antonioni "Michelangelo Antonioni") to China to film the achievements of the Cultural Revolution. Antonioni made the documentary *[Chung Kuo, Cina](/wiki/Chung_Kuo,_Cina "Chung Kuo, Cina")*. When it was released in 1974, CCP leadership in China interpreted the film as [reactionary](/wiki/Reactionary "Reactionary") and anti-Chinese. Viewing art through the principles of the [Yan'an Talks](/wiki/Yan%27an_Forum "Yan'an Forum"), particularly the concept that there is no such thing as art-for-art's-sake, party leadership construed Antonioni's aesthetic choices as politically motivated and banned the film.[[191]](#cite_note-193): 13–14

Mobile film units brought [Chinese cinema](/wiki/Cinema_of_China "Cinema of China") to the countryside and were crucial to the standardization and popularization of culture during this period, particularly including revolutionary model operas.[[176]](#cite_note-Coderre2021-178): 30 During the Cultural Revolution's early years, mobile film teams traveled to rural areas with news reels of Mao meeting with Red Guards and Tiananmen Square parades, which became known as "red treasure films".[[192]](#cite_note-194): 110 The release of the filmed versions of the revolutionary model operas resulted in a re-organization and expansion of China's film exhibition network.[[27]](#cite_note-Li2023-27): 73 From 1965 to 1976, the number of film projection units in China quadrupled, total film audiences nearly tripled, and the national film attendance rate doubled.[[27]](#cite_note-Li2023-27): 133 The Cultural Revolution Group drastically reduced ticket prices which, in its view, would allow film to better serve the needs of workers and of socialism.[[27]](#cite_note-Li2023-27): 133

### Historical sites

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Historical sites")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Trip_to_Ningxia_and_Gansu.jpg/500px-Trip_to_Ningxia_and_Gansu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trip_to_Ningxia_and_Gansu.jpg)

Buddhist statues defaced during the Cultural Revolution

China's historical sites, artifacts and archives suffered devastating damage, as they were thought to be at the root of "old ways of thinking". Artifacts were seized, museums and private homes ransacked, and any item found that was thought to represent bourgeois or [feudal](/wiki/Feudal "Feudal") ideas was destroyed. Few records relate how much was destroyed—Western observers suggest that much of China's thousands of years of history was in effect destroyed, or, later, smuggled abroad for sale. Chinese historians compare the suppression to [Qin Shi Huang](/wiki/Qin_Shi_Huang "Qin Shi Huang")'s [great Confucian purge](/wiki/Great_Confucian_purge "Great Confucian purge"). [Religious persecution](/wiki/Religious_persecution "Religious persecution") intensified during this period, as religion was viewed in opposition to Marxist–Leninist and Maoist thinking.[[44]](#cite_note-Jiaqi-44): 73

The destruction of historical relics was never formally sanctioned by the Party, whose official policy was instead to protect such items. On 14 May 1967, the Central Committee issued *Several suggestions for the protection of cultural relics and books during the Cultural Revolution*.[[162]](#cite_note-Gao-164): 21 Despite this, enormous damage was inflicted on China's cultural heritage.
