For example, a survey in 1972 in Beijing of 18 cultural heritage sites, including the [Temple of Heaven](/wiki/Temple_of_Heaven "Temple of Heaven") and [Ming Tombs](/wiki/Ming_Dynasty_Tombs "Ming Dynasty Tombs"), showed extensive damage. Of the 80 cultural heritage sites in Beijing under municipal protection, 30 were destroyed, and of the 6,843 cultural sites under protection by Beijing government decision in 1958, 4,922 were damaged or destroyed.[[193]](#cite_note-195) Numerous valuable old books, paintings, and other cultural relics were burnt.[[194]](#cite_note-Barnouin-196): 98

Later [archaeological](/wiki/Archaeology "Archaeology") excavation and preservation after the destructive period were protected, and several significant discoveries, such as the [Terracotta Army](/wiki/Terracotta_Army "Terracotta Army") and the [Mawangdui](/wiki/Mawangdui "Mawangdui"), occurred after the peak of the Revolution.[[162]](#cite_note-Gao-164): 21 Nevertheless, the most prominent medium of academic research in archaeology, the journal *[Kaogu](/wiki/Kaogu_(journal) "Kaogu (journal)")*, did not publish.[[195]](#cite_note-197) After the most violent phase, the attack on traditional culture continued in 1973 with the *Anti-Lin Biao, Anti-Confucius Campaign* as part of the struggle against moderate Party elements.

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Media")]

Further information: [Media history of China](/wiki/Media_history_of_China "Media history of China")

During the early period of the Cultural Revolution, [freedom of the press in China](/wiki/Freedom_of_the_press_in_China "Freedom of the press in China") was at its peak.[[196]](#cite_note-Russo2020a-198) While the number of newspapers declined in this period, the number of independent publications by mass political organizations grew.[[197]](#cite_note-Volland2021-199) According to [China's National Bureau of Statistics](/wiki/National_Bureau_of_Statistics_of_China "National Bureau of Statistics of China"), the number of newspapers dropped from 343 in 1965, to 49 in 1966, and then to a 20th-century low of 43 in 1967.[[197]](#cite_note-Volland2021-199) At the same time, the number of publications by mass organizations such as [Red Guards](/wiki/Red_Guards "Red Guards") grew to an estimated number as high as 10,000.[[197]](#cite_note-Volland2021-199)

Independent political groups could publish [broadsheets](/wiki/Broadsheet "Broadsheet") and handbills, as well as leaders' speeches and meeting transcripts which would normally have been considered highly classified.[[46]](#cite_note-Walder2019b-46): 24 From 1966 to 1969, at least 5,000 new broadsheets by independent political groups were published.[[5]](#cite_note-Thornton2019-5): 60 Several Red Guard organizations also operated independent printing presses to publish newspapers, articles, speeches, and [big-character posters](/wiki/Big-character_poster "Big-character poster").[[196]](#cite_note-Russo2020a-198) For example, the largest student organization in Shanghai, the Red Revolutionaries, established a newspaper that had a print run of 800,000 copies by the end of 1966.[[46]](#cite_note-Walder2019b-46): 58–59

## Foreign relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Foreign relations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/1967-09_1967%E5%B9%B4_%E4%B8%AD%E5%9B%BD%E9%A9%BB%E5%8D%B0%E4%BD%BF%E9%A6%86%E8%A2%AB%E7%83%A7%E6%AF%81%E5%90%8E.jpg/250px-1967-09_1967%E5%B9%B4_%E4%B8%AD%E5%9B%BD%E9%A9%BB%E5%8D%B0%E4%BD%BF%E9%A6%86%E8%A2%AB%E7%83%A7%E6%AF%81%E5%90%8E.jpg)](/wiki/File:1967-09_1967%E5%B9%B4_%E4%B8%AD%E5%9B%BD%E9%A9%BB%E5%8D%B0%E4%BD%BF%E9%A6%86%E8%A2%AB%E7%83%A7%E6%AF%81%E5%90%8E.jpg)

The [Embassy of China, Jakarta](/wiki/Embassy_of_China,_Jakarta "Embassy of China, Jakarta") after being burned

The functions of China's embassies abroad were disrupted during the early part of the Cultural Revolution. In a 22 March 1969 meeting on the [Sino-Soviet border clashes](/wiki/Sino-Soviet_border_conflict "Sino-Soviet border conflict"), Mao stated that in [foreign relations](/wiki/History_of_foreign_relations_of_China "History of foreign relations of China"), China was "now isolated" and "we need to relax a little".[[198]](#cite_note-:Li-200): 287 Later that year, China began to restore its embassies to normal functioning.[[198]](#cite_note-:Li-200): 287

However, the [Sino-Soviet conflict](/wiki/Sino-Soviet_conflict "Sino-Soviet conflict") culminated in 1969, and according to declassified documents from both China and the United States, the Soviet Union planned to launch [a large-scale nuclear strike on China](/wiki/Sino-Soviet_split#Planned_Soviet_nuclear_strike_on_China "Sino-Soviet split") after the [Zhenbao Island incident](/wiki/Zhenbao_Island_incident "Zhenbao Island incident") in 1969.[[199]](#cite_note-:3-201)[[200]](#cite_note-:7-202) The planned targets include Beijing, [Changchun](/wiki/Changchun "Changchun"), [Anshan](/wiki/Anshan "Anshan") and China's missile-launch centers of [Jiuquan](/wiki/Jiuquan "Jiuquan"), [Xichang](/wiki/Xichang "Xichang") and [Lop Nur](/wiki/Lop_Nur "Lop Nur").[[199]](#cite_note-:3-201)[[200]](#cite_note-:7-202) This crisis almost led to a major nuclear war, seven years after the [Cuban missile crisis](/wiki/Cuban_Missile_Crisis "Cuban Missile Crisis").[[201]](#cite_note-:11-203) Eventually, the Soviets called off the attack due to the intervention from the United States.[[199]](#cite_note-:3-201)[[200]](#cite_note-:7-202)

China [exported communist revolutions](/wiki/Exporting_the_revolution "Exporting the revolution") as well as communist ideologies to multiple countries in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"), supporting parties in [Indonesia](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Indonesia "Communist Party of Indonesia"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysian_Communist_Party "Malaysian Communist Party"), [Vietnam](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Vietnam "Communist Party of Vietnam"), [Laos](/wiki/Lao_People%27s_Revolutionary_Party "Lao People's Revolutionary Party"), [Myanmar](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Burma "Communist Party of Burma") and the [Khmer Rouge in Cambodia](/wiki/Khmer_Rouge "Khmer Rouge").[[202]](#cite_note-204) It is estimated that at least 90% of the Khmer Rouge's foreign aid came from China. In 1975 alone at least US$1 billion in interest-free economic and military aid and US$20 million came from China.[[203]](#cite_note-205) China's economic malaise impacted China's ability to assist [North Vietnam](/wiki/North_Vietnam "North Vietnam") in its [war](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War") against [South Vietnam](/wiki/South_Vietnam "South Vietnam") by the 1970s, which cooled relations between the once allied nations.[[204]](#cite_note-206)

## Evaluations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cultural_Revolution&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Evaluations")]

Main article: [Evaluation of the Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Evaluation_of_the_Cultural_Revolution "Evaluation of the Cultural Revolution")

On 27 June 1981, the [Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Central_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party") adopted the *[Resolution on Certain Questions in the History of Our Party Since the Founding of the People's Republic of China](/wiki/Resolution_on_Certain_Questions_in_the_History_of_Our_Party_since_the_Founding_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Resolution on Certain Questions in the History of Our Party since the Founding of the People's Republic of China")*, an official assessment of major historical events since 1949.[[205]](#cite_note-marxists.org3-207) The Resolution declared that the Cultural Revolution was "responsible for the most severe setback and the heaviest losses suffered by the people, the country, and the party since the founding of the People's Republic."[[205]](#cite_note-marxists.org3-207)

After the Cultural Revolution, a massive social and cultural movement known as the [New Enlightenment](/wiki/New_Enlightenment_(China) "New Enlightenment (China)") took place in [mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China") since the late 1970s.[[206]](#cite_note-:12-208) The movement lasted throughout the 1980s, and opposed the ideology of Cultural Revolution and [feudalism](/wiki/Feudalism_in_China "Feudalism in China").[[207]](#cite_note-:02-209) The New Enlightenment movement ended due to the [Tiananmen Square protests and massacre](/wiki/1989_Tiananmen_Square_protests_and_massacre "1989 Tiananmen Square protests and massacre") in June 1989.[[208]](#cite_note-:522-210) After [Deng Xiaoping's southern tour](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping%27s_southern_tour "Deng Xiaoping's southern tour") in early 1992, however, intellectuals in mainland China became divided and formed two major [schools of thought](/wiki/School_of_thought "School of thought"), the [*Liberalism*](/wiki/Liberalism_in_China "Liberalism in China") and the [*New Left*](/wiki/Chinese_New_Left "Chinese New Left"), which held different views on the Cultural Revolution.[[207]](#cite_note-:02-209)[[209]](#cite_note-:4022-211) Meanwhile [Maoist](/wiki/Maoism "Maoism") scholars hold another view.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

To this day, public discussion of the Cultural Revolution is still limited within mainland China. The Chinese government continues to prohibit news organizations from mentioning details, and online discussions and books about the topic are subject to official scrutiny. Textbooks abide by the "official view" of the events. Many government documents from the 1960s onward remain classified.[[210]](#cite_note-Fong2-212) Despite inroads by prominent sinologists, independent scholarly research is discouraged.[[210]](#cite_note-Fong2-212)

Mao Zedong's legacy remains in some dispute. During the anniversary of his birth, many people viewed Mao as a godlike figure and referred to him as "the people's great savior". Contemporary discussions in the CCP-owned tabloid *[Global Times](/wiki/Global_Times "Global Times")* continue to glorify Mao. Rather than focus on consequences, state media newspapers claim that revolutions typically have a brutal side and are unable to be viewed from the "humanitarian perspective".[[211]](#cite_note-213) Critics of Mao Zedong look at the actions that occurred under his leadership from the point of view that "he was better at conquering power than at ruling the country and developing a socialist economy". Mao went to extreme measures on his path to power, costing millions of lives then and during his rule.[[212]](#cite_note-214)
